You have a goal — a project, an event, a move, a deadline — and a blank page. Use this when you need to go from "I need to do X" to a clear list of steps you can actually work through.

## The prompt

```
You are an organized project planner helping a non-expert get something done.

My goal: [DESCRIBE YOUR GOAL IN ONE OR TWO SENTENCES]
My situation: [ANY RELEVANT CONSTRAINTS — timeline, budget, who's involved, tools I have]
Output format: a numbered checklist, grouped into logical phases if there are more than 6–8 steps. For each step, add a short note (one line) on why it matters or what to watch out for. Keep language plain and action-oriented.
```

## Before and after

**Before:** "I need to plan a team offsite for 12 people in three weeks. I have no idea where to start."

**After (excerpt):**

> **Phase 1 — Lock the basics (this week)**
> 1. Confirm the date and get attendance commitments — don't book anything until you know who's coming.
> 2. Set a rough budget per person — this drives every other decision.
> 3. Pick a venue type (office, retreat space, restaurant) and book it — good spots fill fast.
>
> **Phase 2 — Agenda and logistics (week 2)**
> 4. Draft a day-of schedule — include breaks; people tire quickly in all-day sessions.
> 5. Arrange travel or parking details and send to attendees...

## Make it yours

- Add "make it shorter" to get a stripped-down 5-step quick-start version.
- Specify your role ("I'm the only person doing this") so the AI doesn't assign tasks to a team you don't have.
- Ask it to flag which steps are blockers — things that hold up everything else.
- Paste your draft checklist back in and ask "what am I missing?" for a second pass.

pages.title: Draft a Reply
pages.description: Write a reply to a message that hits the right note.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've received a message — an email, a Slack thread, a text — and you're not sure how to phrase your response. Maybe the tone is tricky, or you just don't want to stare at a blank reply box.

## The prompt

```
You are a clear, warm professional communicator. I need to reply to the message below.

My goal for this reply: [e.g. confirm I'll attend / push back politely / ask for more time / say yes and add a question]

Tone I want: [e.g. friendly but concise / formal / casual / reassuring]

The original message:
"""
[paste the message here]
"""

Write a reply that:
- Opens naturally (no "I hope this email finds you well")
- Gets to the point in the first sentence
- Stays under [3 sentences / one short paragraph / 150 words — pick one]
- Ends with a clear next step or a warm close, whichever fits

Give me one version. If you're unsure about tone, lean toward warm and direct.
```

## Before and after

**Before:** You received this from your manager: "Hey — can you take the lead on the client debrief Thursday? Let me know."

You want to say yes but flag that you need the client notes first.

**After:**

> Happy to take the lead Thursday. To prepare well, could you send over the client notes beforehand — even rough ones work? Let me know what time works for the call.

## Make it yours

- Add "Keep it under two sentences" if you're a short-reply person.
- Swap in "more formal / less formal" to match your workplace culture.
- Ask for "three options with slightly different tones" if you're not sure which approach to take.
- If the original message has a tricky subtext, describe it in the prompt: "They seem annoyed — I want to acknowledge that without being defensive."

## Watch out for

AI tends to over-agree. If you need to push back or set a limit, say so clearly in your goal. Otherwise the reply may come out softer than you intended. Always read it once before sending: the AI doesn't know your relationship with this person.

pages.title: Write a Polite No
pages.description: Decline a request kindly but clearly.
pages.body:
## When to use this

Someone asks you to do something you can't or don't want to do: take on a project, attend an event, give a favor. You want to say no without leaving them feeling dismissed, or leaving yourself feeling guilty about the wording.

## The prompt

```
You are a thoughtful communicator who is skilled at declining requests with warmth and clarity.

I need to say no to the following request:
"""
[describe or paste the request]
"""

My reason (share only what I'm comfortable sharing): [e.g. I'm at capacity / it's not the right fit / I don't have the expertise / personal reasons — no need to over-explain]

Relationship to this person: [e.g. colleague / client / acquaintance / friend]

Write a short, kind decline that:
- Acknowledges the ask without being effusive
- States the no clearly — no waffling or "maybe later" if I mean no
- Offers a small gesture if appropriate (a referral, a brief reason, a door left open) — but only if it fits naturally; don't force it
- Ends on a warm note
- Stays under 100 words

Don't start with "I" and don't use "unfortunately" or "I'm afraid."
```

## Before and after

**Before:** A former colleague emails asking if you can review their business plan this week. You don't have time and it's outside your area.

**After:**

> Thanks for thinking of me — sounds like an exciting project. This week is fully booked on my end, and honestly the financial modeling side isn't my strongest suit. For that piece, someone like [name] might be a better fit. Wishing you real momentum on it.

## Make it yours

- Add "leave the door open for [specific future thing]" if you want to keep the relationship warm.
- Say "make it shorter — two sentences only" if brevity is your style.
- If the request is recurring (someone keeps asking), add: "This is the third time they've asked — I want to be gently firm without being rude."
- Ask for "a more formal version" when writing to a client or someone senior.

pages.title: Extract Key Details from Text
pages.description: Pull specific fields out of unstructured text.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have a block of text: an email, a contract clause, a product description, a support ticket. You need specific fields pulled out of it without reading the whole thing yourself. Useful when you have one piece of text, or a handful, and need the same fields from each.

## The prompt

```
You are a detail extractor. I will give you a block of text and a list of fields I need. Your job is to pull exactly those fields from the text and return them as a simple list.

Fields I need:
[LIST YOUR FIELDS HERE — one per line, e.g. "Sender name", "Deadline", "Requested amount", "Next step"]

Rules:
- Use only what is explicitly stated in the text. Do not guess or fill in gaps.
- If a field is not present, write "Not mentioned" next to it.
- Keep each answer short — a phrase or a date, not a full sentence.
- Do not summarize or rephrase anything else.

Text to extract from:
[PASTE YOUR TEXT HERE]
```

## Before and after

**Before** — an email excerpt:

> Hi team, just a reminder that the invoice from Apex Supplies ($4,200) needs to be approved by the end of day Friday. Please route to Marcus in finance. Let me know if you have questions — Jamie <!-- noev: worked example -->

Fields requested: Vendor name · Amount · Deadline · Approver · Sender

**After** — what the AI returns:

- **Vendor name:** Apex Supplies
- **Amount:** $4,200 <!-- noev: worked example -->
- **Deadline:** End of day Friday
- **Approver:** Marcus (finance)
- **Sender:** Jamie
- **Not mentioned:** (all fields found)

## Make it yours

- Add "format the output as a markdown table" if you're extracting from multiple texts and want to compare them side by side.
- Specify a format for certain fields: "Return dates as YYYY-MM-DD."
- If extracting from contracts or legal text, add: "quote the exact sentence the answer comes from."
- Paste several texts in numbered blocks and ask it to produce one row per text.

## Watch out for

A field can appear multiple times with different values. Say two deadlines are mentioned in one email: the AI may pick one and silently drop the other. If precision matters, ask it to "list all occurrences" for fields that might repeat. It also won't catch information that's implied rather than stated. If the deadline is "before the weekend" in an email sent Thursday, it won't infer Friday.

pages.title: Turn Messy Notes into a Table
pages.description: Convert a pile of notes into a clean, structured table.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have a scattered list of notes, ideas, or bullet points and you need them organized so you can compare, sort, or share them. This works well after a meeting, a brainstorm session, or any time your notes feel like a pile rather than a plan.

## The prompt

```
You are a data organizer. I have a set of notes below and I want you to turn them into a clean table.

Steps:
1. Read through all the notes and identify the key categories or fields (for example: Name, Date, Action, Owner).
2. Create a markdown table with one row per item and a column for each field you identified.
3. Fill in each cell using only what's in the notes — do not guess or invent information. If a field is missing for an item, leave that cell blank.
4. After the table, list any items you weren't sure how to categorize.

Here are my notes:
[PASTE YOUR NOTES HERE]
```

## Before and after

**Before** — raw notes from a planning call:

- Sarah will follow up with the vendor by Thursday
- Need to book the conference room for the 14th, Tom owns this
- Budget approval still pending — finance team
- Dev environment setup, no owner yet, should be done before end of month

**After** — what the AI returns:

| Task | Owner | Due Date | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| Follow up with vendor | Sarah | Thursday | |
| Book conference room | Tom | 14th | |
| Budget approval | Finance team | | Pending |
| Dev environment setup | | End of month | |

## Make it yours

- Add "add a Priority column (High / Medium / Low) based on the urgency implied in the notes" to the prompt.
- Ask it to sort the table by due date or owner after building it.
- If your notes are in a specific format (email thread, Slack dump), tell it: "these are messages from a Slack channel."
- Ask it to output CSV instead of a markdown table if you're pasting into a spreadsheet.

## Watch out for

The AI will occasionally merge two separate items into one row, or split one item into two. Skim the table against your original notes before sharing it. Also, it can only assign an owner if the name appears in your notes; it will not infer who should own something.

pages.title: Pull Out Action Items
pages.description: Extract the to-dos and decisions from notes or a transcript.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have meeting notes, a call transcript, or a messy email thread, and you need to know: who said they'd do what, and by when? Use this to turn a wall of text into a clean list you can actually act on.

## The prompt

```
You are a meticulous project coordinator. I'm going to paste meeting notes or a transcript below.

Please extract:
1. **Action items** — one per line, in the format: [Person responsible] · [What they will do] · [Deadline, if mentioned]
2. **Decisions made** — brief bullet list of anything the group agreed on
3. **Open questions** — things that came up but weren't resolved

If a person's name isn't clear, use a description like "the facilitator" or "the sales lead."
If no deadline was mentioned for an action item, write "no deadline set."

Do not add items that weren't in the text. If a category has nothing, write "None."

Here are the notes:

[PASTE YOUR NOTES OR TRANSCRIPT HERE]
```

## Before and after

**Before:** A block of rough meeting notes from a 45-minute product review:

> "Sara said she'd look into the API rate limits by end of week. Tom needs to send the updated specs to the dev team — he mentioned Thursday. We agreed to push the launch to Q3. Still unclear whether we need legal sign-off on the new data fields. Marcus is going to check with compliance, no specific date given."

**After:**

> **Action items:**
> - Sara · Research API rate limits · Friday
> - Tom · Send updated specs to dev team · Thursday
> - Marcus · Check with compliance re: new data fields · No deadline set
>
> **Decisions made:**
> - Launch pushed to Q3
>
> **Open questions:**
> - Whether legal sign-off is required for the new data fields

## Make it yours

- **Slack or email thread:** Paste the whole thread and ask it to pull items from everyone's messages.
- **Owner focus:** Add "Only show items assigned to [your name]" to filter the list.
- **Formatted output:** Ask for a markdown table with columns: Owner | Task | Deadline | Status (set all to "open").
- **Follow-up email draft:** After getting the list, say "Now draft a short follow-up email to the team recapping these items."

## Watch out for

AI can only find what's written down. If someone said they'd "handle it" without specifics, AI will flag it vaguely — you'll need to clarify ownership yourself. Also check for implied commitments ("I'll circle back") that the AI might miss or misread as firm action items.

pages.title: Summarize a Document
pages.description: Get the key points of a long document in seconds.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have a long document — a report, contract, article, or meeting brief — and you need the gist before a call or decision. Paste it in and skip the 45-minute read.

## The prompt

```
You are a sharp executive assistant. I'm going to paste a document below. Please summarize it for me.

Return:
- A 2–3 sentence overview of what this document is about
- 5 key points, each in one sentence
- Any decisions, deadlines, or action items buried in the text (list them separately, or say "none found")

Keep the language plain and direct. Do not add commentary or opinions — just extract what's there.

Here is the document:

[PASTE YOUR DOCUMENT HERE]
```

## Before and after

**Before:** A 12-page vendor proposal about a new software platform, full of marketing language, feature tables, and pricing appendices. You have 10 minutes before the vendor call.

**After:**

> **Overview:** This is a proposal from Apex Systems for a workflow automation platform aimed at mid-size operations teams. The proposal covers three pricing tiers and a 90-day implementation timeline.
>
> **Key points:**
> 1. Base tier starts at $1,200/month for up to 50 users. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> 2. Full API access requires the Enterprise tier.
> 3. A dedicated onboarding manager is included in all tiers.
> 4. Data migration support is limited to structured formats (CSV, Excel).
> 5. Contract is annual with a 30-day cancellation window after year one.
>
> **Decisions / deadlines:** Proposal expires July 15. Pilot program slots are limited to 3 new clients this quarter.

## Make it yours

- **Shorter:** Add "Give me a 3-bullet TL;DR — no more." to the top of the prompt.
- **Specific angle:** Replace the key points instruction with "Focus only on the financial terms and commitments."
- **Multiple documents:** Paste two documents separated by a divider and ask it to compare them on specific dimensions.
- **Jargon-heavy text:** Add "Define any technical terms the first time you use them."

## Watch out for

AI can miss nuance buried in footnotes or tables — always check the actual document for anything you'll sign or commit to. If the document is very long (over 20 pages), paste it in sections and ask for a rolling summary.

pages.title: Draft an Email
pages.description: Turn a few bullet points into a finished email you can send.
pages.body:
## When to use this

When you know what you want to say but the blank page is staring back at you. Works especially well when the tone needs care. Think following up, asking a favour, delivering bad news, or writing to someone you don't know well.

## The prompt

```
You are a clear, professional writer helping me draft an email.

Context:
- Who I'm writing to: [name/role, e.g. "my manager, Sarah"]
- Our relationship: [e.g. "I report to her, friendly but professional"]
- What I need to communicate: [paste your bullet points here]
- Tone I'm going for: [e.g. "warm but direct", "formal", "friendly and casual"]
- Any constraints: [e.g. "keep it under 150 words", "don't mention the budget yet"]

Write a complete email with a subject line. Use plain, natural language — no corporate buzzwords. End with a clear next step or call to action if one makes sense.
```

## Before and after

**Before** — raw notes dropped into the prompt:

- Need to reschedule Thursday's 2pm call with Marcus
- My daughter's school thing — can't get out of it
- Want to keep the momentum going on the project
- Maybe next week? His schedule seems full Mondays

**After** — what the AI returns:

> **Subject:** Rescheduling Thursday's call
>
> Hi Marcus,
>
> Something's come up on Thursday afternoon that I can't move — I need to reschedule our 2pm call. Sorry for the short notice.
>
> Would any slot next Tuesday or Wednesday work for you? I want to keep the project moving, so I'm flexible on timing.
>
> Let me know what suits you.
>
> [Your name]

## Make it yours

- **Change the length:** Add "Keep it under 100 words" or "This can be longer — cover all the detail."
- **Change the register:** Swap "warm but direct" for "very formal" or "casual, like texting a friend."
- **Reply to a chain:** Paste the email you received into the prompt and add "I'm replying to the email below."
- **Multiple versions:** Ask for "three different subject lines" or "a softer version and a more direct version."

## Watch out for

AI is good at finding the right words, but it doesn't know your relationship with the reader the way you do. Read what it gives you before you send. Check the tone, trim anything that sounds off, and make sure any commitments it made are ones you're actually prepared to keep.

pages.title: Write Social Posts & Captions
pages.description: Generate platform-ready posts and captions from one idea.
pages.body:
## When to use this

When you have something to share but can't find the right words, or you need the same idea formatted for multiple platforms at once. Works well for product announcements, event promos, behind-the-scenes moments, or just staying active when inspiration is thin.

## The prompt

```
You are a social media copywriter who writes in a natural, human voice — no hollow hype or cringe hashtag dumps.

Here's my idea: [describe what you want to share in 1–3 sentences]

My brand/personal voice is: [e.g. "friendly and direct", "dry and witty", "warm and encouraging"]

Please write posts for the following platforms:
- X: one punchy post, max 280 characters. No hashtags unless they genuinely add value.
- LinkedIn: a short update, max 3,000 characters. Professional but not stuffy. Start with a hook, not "I'm excited to announce."
- Instagram: a caption with a clear first line (the bit shown before "more"), then the rest of the caption. Add 3–5 relevant hashtags at the end.

Keep each platform's post feeling native to that platform — different length, different energy.
```

## Before and after

**Before** — the idea pasted into the prompt:

> We just shipped a small feature that lets users save their favourite templates. Took two weeks but our team is really proud of it. Voice: friendly and real.

**After** — excerpt of what the AI returns:

> **X:**
> We shipped something small today that I think you'll actually use — save your favourite templates. Two weeks of work. Worth it. 🎉
>
> **LinkedIn:**
> Two weeks ago, we set out to solve a small but annoying problem: having to rebuild the same template every time.
>
> Today we shipped it — you can now save your favourite templates and pull them up instantly.
>
> It's a small thing. But it's the kind of small thing that adds up.
>
> **Instagram:**
> Save the ones you love. ✨
>
> You can now save your favourite templates and reuse them whenever you need them — no rebuilding from scratch.
>
> This one took two weeks and a lot of iteration. We're really happy with how it turned out.
>
> \#productupdate #ux #buildinginpublic #saas #newfeature

## Know the limits

Platform limits matter — the AI won't always get them right on the first try, so check before you post.

- **X (Twitter):** Standard posts are capped at **280 characters** ([X Developer Docs, 2026](#ev-x-post-character-limit-280)). Keep it tight, or break a longer thought into a thread.
- **LinkedIn:** Posts can be up to **3,000 characters** ([LinkedIn Help, 2026](#ev-linkedin-post-character-limit-3000)). Anything longer needs to be published as an article instead.
- **Instagram hashtags:** You can use up to **5 hashtags** per post ([Instagram Help Center, 2026](#ev-instagram-hashtag-limit-5)). Instagram cut this from 30 to 5, saying fewer, well-chosen hashtags perform better. So the prompt asking for 3–5 is right where you want to be.
- **Caption length:** Instagram's caption character limit is not clearly documented in their Help Center. In practice, captions are cut off in the feed after the first 125 characters — make your first line count.

## Make it yours

- **One platform only:** Remove the other platforms from the prompt and ask for three variations with different tones instead.
- **Add your own examples:** Drop in a recent post that performed well and say "match this energy."
- **Thread format:** For X, ask for "a 5-post thread instead of a single post."
- **Scheduling copy:** Add "also write a short note I can use as the caption when I schedule this in a social tool."

## Sources

<!-- generated from validation/evidence.yaml — do not edit; run scripts/build_index.py -->

pages.title: The Leadership Map
pages.description: How this sub-domain is organized — six leadership functions and what each is for.
pages.body:
This page is the map. It shows how **Executive Leadership** is organized so you can go straight to the work in front of you — a board update due Friday, a budget review to prep, a hard conversation you've been putting off.

## Organized by the job a leader owns

The pages are grouped by **leadership function** — the kind of work, not your exact title. A CEO, CFO, and COO share most of these jobs; you'll dip into whichever one matches the task at hand. There's no order to work through. Jump to the function you need:

- **[Strategy & Planning](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/strategy-planning/index.md)** — set direction, scan the field, pressure-test the plan
- **[Finance & Performance](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/finance-performance/index.md)** — read the numbers and prep the reviews
- **[People & Org](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/people-org/index.md)** — org and hiring calls, and the conversations that come with them
- **[Board & Investor](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/board-investor/index.md)** — updates and the Q&A that follows
- **[Communications](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/communications/index.md)** — the messages a leader sends, routine and high-stakes
- **[Decisions & Meetings](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/decisions-meetings/index.md)** — make the call explicit and run the room

## Two kinds of page

Leadership work isn't all one shape, so the pages aren't either:

- **Recipes** hand you a ready-to-paste prompt for a concrete task — a board update, a one-page read of a finance deck, a decision memo. Copy it, drop in your details, run it.
- **Workflows** walk you through a judgment-heavy job — an org decision, a scenario stress-test, a crisis message — where the value is in the thinking, not a single prompt. They show where AI helps and where you stay in the driver's seat.

Every page is built around *doing the thing*, not reading about it.

## What stays yours

AI drafts fast, and it is sometimes confidently wrong. On leadership work the stakes are higher than a typo: a number in a board deck, the tone of a layoff message, the framing of a strategic bet. Treat every output as a strong first draft — check the facts that matter, and keep the final wording and the final call your own. The [glossary](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/glossary.md) explains the few terms used here.

pages.title: Draft a Board Update
pages.description: Turn the quarter's facts into a clear, honest board update.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have the quarter's facts — the revenue number, the headcount you added, the deal that slipped, the launch that landed — and a board update due. The facts aren't the hard part. The hard part is shaping them into something a board can read in a few minutes and trust. That means being clear on what went well, honest about what didn't, and specific about what you need from them.

Use this when you have the raw material and want a strong first draft to edit, not a blank page to fill.

## The prompt

```
You are helping a CEO write a clear, honest update to their board of directors. Turn the facts below into a board update.

Structure it as:
1. TL;DR — three or four sentences a busy director can read first: how the quarter went overall, and the one or two things you most need them to know.
2. Highlights — what went well, with the numbers.
3. Lowlights / risks — what went wrong or is at risk, stated plainly. Do not soften or spin these.
4. Key metrics — a short table of the numbers I give you, this period vs. last.
5. Asks — specific things I need from the board (intros, a decision, advice).

Rules:
- Use only the facts I provide. Do not invent numbers, metrics, or context. If something important seems missing, list it under "Gaps to fill" at the end instead of guessing.
- Plain, direct language. No hype, no jargon, no filler.
- Honest tone — a board that trusts your bad news will trust your good news.

Facts for this quarter:
[PASTE YOUR RAW NOTES — revenue, growth, hires, departures, wins, misses, product, cash/runway, what you need. Bullet points are fine.]
```

## Before and after

**Before — raw notes you'd paste in:**
> - Revenue $1.2M this Q, was $950K last Q <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Signed BigCo, our biggest deal ever
> - Lost two engineers, hiring is slow
> - Mobile launch slipped to next Q, my fault, underestimated it
> - Cash: ~14 months runway
> - Need 2 intros to potential VP Eng candidates

**After — the kind of update it produces:**

> **TL;DR.** Strong quarter on revenue — up 26% to $1.2M, led by our largest-ever deal (BigCo). The one thing to flag: our mobile launch slipped a quarter, and engineering hiring is behind. Runway is healthy at ~14 months. My main ask: intros to two VP Engineering candidates. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **Highlights**
> - Revenue grew to $1.2M, up from $950K last quarter (+26%). <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Closed BigCo — our largest contract to date.
>
> **Lowlights / risks**
> - The mobile launch slipped to next quarter. I underestimated the scope; that's on me.
> - We lost two engineers and hiring has been slow, which is what put mobile at risk.
>
> **Key metrics**
>
> | Metric | This quarter | Last quarter |
> |---|---|---|
> | Revenue | $1.2M | $950K | <!-- noev: worked example -->
> | Runway | ~14 months | — |
>
> **Asks**
> - Two intros to strong VP Engineering candidates.
>
> **Gaps to fill**
> - No prior-quarter runway given, so the trend isn't shown.
> - No customer or retention numbers included — add if the board tracks them.

## Make it yours

- **Set the length.** Add *"Keep it under one page"* or *"Give me a tight version and a detailed version."*
- **Match how your board reads.** *"My board prefers a narrative to a metrics table — write the metrics into prose."*
- **Pre-empt the hard questions.** Add *"After the update, list the three toughest questions a director might ask, so I can prepare."* (See [Prep Board / Investor Q&A](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/board-investor/prep-board-investor-qa.md).)
- **Reuse your format.** Paste last quarter's update and add *"Match this structure and tone."*

## Watch out for

- **Check every number against the source.** A board update is the wrong place for a transcription slip. The prompt is told not to invent figures, but it can still copy one wrong — verify each against your actual numbers before you send.
- **You decide what to disclose, not the tool.** What goes to a board — and how a risk is framed — is your judgment and sometimes your counsel's. Treat the draft as a starting point, not a disclosure decision.
- **Don't let it smooth over bad news.** AI tends to soften. Read the lowlights and make sure the slip, the miss, or the risk still reads as plainly as it should. That honesty is what earns a board's trust.

pages.title: Prep Board / Investor Q&A
pages.description: Anticipate the hard questions a board or investor will ask, and your answers.
pages.body:
## When to use this

The update is written. What you can't script is the Q&A. A director asks why margins slipped; an investor wants the real story behind the churn number. Use this before a board meeting or investor call to surface the hard questions in advance and rehearse answers you actually believe.

This is not a one-shot prompt. It's a back-and-forth: you treat the AI as a skeptical board member, it presses, you answer, it presses harder. The goal is to find the questions that would catch you flat-footed while you still have time to think.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop with something like this, then keep the conversation going:

```
You are a sharp, skeptical board member preparing for my upcoming board meeting. I'll paste my update below. Your job is to find the weak spots.

First, list the 8–10 hardest questions a board or investor would ask after reading this — the ones I'd least want to get. Rank them by how uncomfortable they are. Then wait.

I'll answer them one at a time. After each answer, push back the way a real director would: probe what I dodged, ask for the number behind the claim, or point out where I'm being vague. Don't let me off easy. Don't accept a non-answer.

My update:
[PASTE YOUR BOARD UPDATE OR KEY POINTS]
```

## Work through it step by step

1. **Get the question list first.** Read the questions before you answer any. Some you'll have ready; the ones that make you wince are where the prep pays off.
2. **Answer out loud, then type.** Say your answer as you would in the room, then paste it in. You're rehearsing delivery, not drafting a memo.
3. **Let it push back.** When it challenges an answer, don't defend — fix. If it caught a dodge, your real director will too. Tighten the answer until the pushback stops landing.
4. **Hunt for the number behind each claim.** If you say growth is "strong," it should ask "compared to what?" Have the figure ready, and check it against the source.
5. **Find the one question you can't answer well.** There's usually one. That's the most valuable output of the session. Now you can prepare a real answer, line up the data, or decide how to handle it honestly in the room.

## What stays your call

The answers have to be *yours* — your real positions, your real commitments. The AI is good at generating questions and stress-testing phrasing. It has no idea what you can actually deliver next quarter, or what you're willing to promise a board. Use it to find the questions and pressure-test the wording. Never let it talk you into a commitment you don't mean, or a confident-sounding answer you can't stand behind when you're asked it for real.

## Watch out for

- **A smooth answer is not a true one.** AI will help you phrase almost anything persuasively, including things that aren't right. Rehearse honesty, not spin — a board remembers the answer that turned out to be wrong.
- **Keep sensitive specifics out of a tool you don't control.** Named personnel issues, unannounced deals, legal exposure — don't paste those into a chat tool. Rehearse those questions in general terms, or offline.
- **It will invent plausible board questions that don't fit your situation.** Use the list as a prompt for your own judgment, not a script. You know your board; drop the questions that don't apply and add the ones it missed.

pages.title: Handle a Sensitive or Crisis Message
pages.description: Work through a sensitive or crisis message carefully before it goes out.
pages.body:
## When to use this

Some messages can't be taken back. A layoff note, a response to a breach, an apology after something went wrong. One careless line here does real, lasting damage. So this is the page where you slow down on purpose.

Use it when the message is sensitive, the audience is upset or anxious, and the stakes are high. The AI is a sounding board, not the author. You own every word, and you own the decision to send.

This isn't a single prompt. It's a slow loop: get clear, draft, test how it reads, revise, then check who else must see it first.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop with something like this. Then keep the conversation going — don't rush to a final draft.

```
I'm preparing a sensitive message and I want to get it right, not get it fast.

Here's the situation: [WHAT HAPPENED, IN PLAIN TERMS]
Who's receiving this: [AUDIENCE — and how they likely feel right now]
What I need them to understand: [THE ONE OR TWO THINGS THAT MATTER MOST]
What I can't say yet, or shouldn't: [ANYTHING LEGAL, UNCONFIRMED, OR PRIVATE]

Before we draft anything, ask me the questions you'd need to write this well.
Don't soften the situation. Don't reassure where reassurance isn't earned.
```

## Work through it step by step

1. **Get clear on the facts and the audience.** Answer its questions honestly. Be sure of what's true before you shape how to say it. Know who's reading and what they fear. A message that ignores the reader's worry won't land.

2. **Draft, slowly.** Ask for a first draft, then read it as the recipient. Does it acknowledge what happened? Or does it dodge? A direct, honest opening beats a warm one that buries the point.

3. **Test how each line could be read — and misread.** Go line by line. Ask: "How might an anxious or angry reader take this?" Hunt for any sentence that sounds evasive, cold, or like it's hiding something.

4. **Revise for honesty, not comfort.** Cut the spin. If something needs a plain acknowledgement, give it one. Don't let the draft smooth over a hard truth just because that feels safer — it rarely is.

5. **Decide who else must see it before it goes.** Most sensitive messages aren't yours alone to send. Loop in comms, HR, or legal. Ask yourself who gets blindsided if this goes out — and bring them in first.

6. **Sit with it, then decide.** Read it once more, out loud if you can. If a line still nags at you, fix it or hold the message. Send only when you'd stand behind every word.

## What stays your call

Every word is yours. So is the timing, and the choice to send at all.

The AI can help you see how a line lands and where you're being vague. It can't know what's actually true, what you can legally say, or what your people need to hear from you directly. It has no stake in the outcome — you do.

Use it to pressure-test and to slow down. Never let it make the call.

## Watch out for

- **Keep sensitive specifics out of a tool you don't control.** Names, legal exposure, unconfirmed details, anything private — don't paste those into a chat tool. Work in general terms, or take it offline.
- **For a real crisis, involve comms, legal, or counsel.** This workflow helps you think. It doesn't replace the people whose job is to protect the company and the people in it.
- **AI tends to smooth and reassure.** It will round off hard edges and offer comfort by default. Don't let it paper over something that needs a direct, honest acknowledgement. Sometimes the right message is the harder one.

pages.title: Write an All-Hands or Exec Message
pages.description: Draft a clear, on-tone all-hands or company-wide message.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have something to tell the whole company. A win, a change, a quarter wrapping up, a new direction. You know the key points. You just need them shaped into a message people will read and feel good about.

The hard part isn't the facts. It's the tone. The same news lands differently as a warm note or a flat memo.

Use this when you want a strong first draft to react to. You'll still write the final version. This gets you off the blank page fast.

## The prompt

```
You are helping a CEO write a company-wide message. Turn the points below into a clear, on-tone message to the whole team.

Tone I want: [WARM AND PERSONAL / CALM AND STEADY / DIRECT AND BRIEF / CELEBRATORY — pick one or describe it]

Key points to cover:
[LIST THE POINTS — what happened, what's changing, what people should do or feel. Bullets are fine.]

Context the team already knows:
[ANYTHING THEY'VE ALREADY HEARD, so you don't over-explain. Optional.]

Write it as:
1. An opening line that gets to the point — no throat-clearing.
2. The body — the points above, in an order that flows.
3. A close — what happens next, or what I want people to take away.

Rules:
- Use only what I gave you. Don't invent news, numbers, or promises. If something seems missing, list it under "Worth adding?" at the end instead of guessing.
- Plain, human language. No corporate filler, no hype, no "synergy."
- Sound like a person talking to their team, not a press release.
```

## Before and after

**Before — the blunt points you'd paste in:**

> - We hit our annual revenue goal a quarter early <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Want to thank the team, especially sales and support
> - Reminder: we're not slowing down, next goal is bigger
> - Tone: warm but not over the top

**After — the kind of message it produces:**

> **We hit our number — a full quarter early.**
>
> I wanted you to hear this from me first. We just crossed our revenue goal for the year, with a quarter to spare. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> That's not a spreadsheet milestone. It's the result of a lot of hard, often invisible work. Sales closed it. Support kept every customer we won. And everyone behind them made that possible.
>
> So, thank you. Genuinely.
>
> Here's the honest part: we're not coasting into the holidays. Hitting this early means we get to aim higher next year, and I think that's the fun part. More on what that looks like soon.
>
> For now — take the win. You earned it.

## Make it yours

- **Shift the tone.** Swap the tone line and re-run. *"Make it calmer"* or *"more celebratory"* changes the whole feel.
- **Match how you actually talk.** Paste an old message of yours and add *"Write it in this voice."*
- **Set the length.** Add *"Keep it to four short paragraphs"* or *"give me a 30-second version too."*
- **Pick the channel.** *"This is going in Slack, not email — make it shorter and looser."*
- **Pressure-test it.** Add *"How might this be misread? Flag any line that could land wrong."*

## Watch out for

- **Read it aloud before you send.** A leader's words carry further than anyone's. If a line doesn't sound like you when you say it, rewrite it. The final wording is yours, not the tool's.
- **AI drifts into generic corporate tone.** It loves "excited to announce" and "incredible journey." Cut those. Your team can tell the difference between you and a template.
- **Make sure it fits the moment.** A warm, upbeat draft is wrong for hard news. Match the tone to what people are actually feeling, not to what reads nicely.
- **You own every word.** People will quote this back to you. The draft is a starting point — edit it until it sounds like you.

pages.title: Prep and Run a Leadership Meeting
pages.description: Build an agenda and pre-read so a leadership meeting drives to decisions.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've got a leadership meeting coming up. The room is full of expensive people. Too often that hour turns into a round of status updates — everyone reports, no one decides. You leave with notes, not calls.

Use this when you know what the meeting needs to settle and who's in the room. You bring the topics and the decisions that must be made. The prompt turns them into a tight agenda with time boxes and a short pre-read, so people walk in ready to decide.

## The prompt

```
You are helping a leader plan a leadership meeting that drives to decisions, not status updates. Turn the topics below into an agenda.

For the agenda:
1. Open with the decisions this meeting must reach — list them at the top, stated as questions to answer.
2. Order topics so the most important decision gets time while everyone is fresh.
3. Give each topic a time box and name what it's for: a decision, a discussion, or an FYI. Most slots should be decisions.
4. For each decision topic, note who owns the call and what the group needs from the room.
5. End with a few minutes to confirm decisions made and owners for next steps.

Then write a short pre-read outline — a few bullets per topic covering the context and the choice on the table — so people arrive ready to decide instead of being briefed live.

Rules:
- Use only the topics I give you. If a topic is too vague to time-box, say so and ask what it needs to decide.
- Cut anything that's just a status update unless a decision hangs on it.
- Plain language. No filler.

Topics for this meeting:
[LIST YOUR TOPICS — and, for each, what must be decided. Rough notes are fine.]

Who's in the room: [ROLES — roles are safer than names in a tool you don't control]
Total time: [E.G. 60 MIN]
```

## Before and after

**Before — the vague topic list you'd paste in:**
> - Q3 hiring plan <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Marketing update
> - The pricing thing
> - Roadmap
> - Office lease

**After — the kind of agenda it produces:**

> **Decisions this meeting must reach**
> 1. Do we approve the Q3 hiring plan, or trim it? <!-- noev: worked example -->
> 2. Do we move to the new pricing tier this quarter or next?
>
> **Agenda (60 min)** <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - **Pricing change — decide (20 min).** Owner: Head of Product. The group needs to weigh churn risk against revenue. Decision: this quarter or next. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - **Q3 hiring plan — decide (15 min).** Owner: you. Approve as proposed, or cut roles to protect runway. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - **Roadmap — discuss (15 min).** No call today; surface the one trade-off we'll decide next time. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - **Marketing + office lease — FYI (5 min).** Read in the pre-read; flag only if blocked. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - **Confirm decisions and owners (5 min).** <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **Pre-read outline**
> - *Pricing:* current tier, proposed tier, the churn-vs-revenue trade-off, what changes if we wait.
> - *Hiring:* roles, cost, runway impact at full plan vs. trimmed.

## Make it yours

- **Tighten the clock.** Add *"This is a 30-minute meeting"* and it'll cut or merge topics to fit.
- **Force a pre-mortem.** Add *"For the biggest decision, list two ways it could go wrong"* so the room walks in with the risks already named.
- **Name the deciders.** Paste who's in the room and add *"Flag any decision where the right owner isn't present"* — better to catch that before the meeting than during.
- **Reuse your shape.** Paste a past agenda that worked and add *"Match this structure."*

## Watch out for

- **An agenda doesn't run the room for you.** This page produces the prep — a sharp agenda and a pre-read. Driving the conversation to an actual decision is still your job. When discussion drifts, you're the one who pulls it back to the call on the table.
- **The tool can't know your real priorities.** It orders topics from what you typed, not from what's actually weighing on the business. Read the agenda back and confirm it leads with what truly must be decided. Reorder it if not.
- **Watch for status creep.** Even a decision-first agenda can fill with updates if you let topics in that don't need a call. If something's just an FYI, keep it in the pre-read and out of the room's time.

pages.title: Prioritize Across Competing Demands
pages.description: Work through competing priorities to a defensible ranking.
pages.body:
## When to use this

Everything feels urgent. The board wants growth, the team wants stability, and three projects all claim to be the priority. You can't do all of them well. Use this when you need to rank competing demands and be able to defend the order out loud.

This isn't a one-shot prompt. It's a loop. You name what's competing, you name what actually matters to you, and the AI helps lay the trade-offs side by side. Then you test rankings until one feels right. The tool organizes the tension. You make the call.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop with something like this, then keep going turn by turn:

```
I'm trying to rank some competing priorities and I want to think it through with you, not have you decide for me.

Here's what's on my plate right now:
[LIST EVERYTHING COMPETING FOR YOUR TIME, MONEY, OR ATTENTION]

The things I care about most when I weigh these:
[NAME YOUR REAL CRITERIA — e.g. revenue impact, risk, team morale, what I can actually staff]

Help me lay these out as trade-offs. Don't rank them yet. First, ask me what I'm missing, then show me where my criteria conflict. I'll take it from there.
```

## Work through it step by step

1. **List everything that's competing.** Get it all out, even the small things. Ask the AI to play back the list and flag anything you lumped together that's really two things.
2. **Name the criteria that actually matter.** Not the ones that sound good. The real ones. Revenue, risk, who you'd have to pull off other work. Be honest about what drives your decisions.
3. **Weight them.** Some criteria matter more than others. Tell the AI which ones win when two collide. This is the step people skip, and it's the one that decides the answer.
4. **Test a ranking.** Ask it to order the list using your weights. Read the result. Where does it feel wrong? That gut reaction is data.
5. **Adjust until it feels right.** When a ranking surprises you, dig in. Either a weight was off, or your gut is wrong and worth examining. Tweak and re-run until the order holds up.
6. **Say it out loud.** Walk the final ranking like you're defending it to your team. If you can't explain why number three beats number four, you're not done.

## What stays your call

The ranking is yours. So are the criteria behind it. The AI can sort, weight, and re-sort all day, but it doesn't know your business. It can't tell that a quiet retention risk matters more to you than an obvious growth bet. It doesn't carry the consequences. Use it to surface the trade-offs and pressure-test the order. Never let a tidy ranking stand in for a decision you haven't actually made.

## Watch out for

- **The criteria and the ranking are your judgment.** The tool organizes the trade-offs. It can't know what matters most to your business. If the inputs are wrong, the order is wrong, no matter how clean it looks.
- **A clean-looking ranking can hide a bad assumption.** When the output looks neat, check the weights, not just the order. The math is only as good as what you told it to value.
- **It will sound confident about a call it can't make.** A ranking can come back clean and reasonable and still be wrong for you. When it disagrees with your instinct, check why before you accept it.

pages.title: Write a Decision Memo
pages.description: Turn a pending call into a crisp decision memo others can act on.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have a call to make. You've turned it over in your head, talked to a few people, and you're close to a decision. But it lives in your notes and your inbox, not on a page anyone else can follow.

A decision memo fixes that. It states the call, the reasoning behind it, and what happens next — in one page others can act on. Use this when you've done the thinking and want a clean draft to sharpen. The decision is yours; the tool just drafts it.

## The prompt

```
You are helping an executive write a clear decision memo. Turn the decision and context below into a structured memo.

Structure it as:
1. The decision — one or two sentences. State the call plainly, up front.
2. Why — the reasoning behind it, in three or four points.
3. Options considered — the main alternatives, each with a one-line "why not."
4. Risks and what would change my mind — the real downsides, and the signals that would make me reconsider.
5. What happens next — the concrete steps, who owns each, and by when.

Rules:
- Use only what I give you. Do not invent facts, numbers, owners, or dates. If something important is missing, list it under "Open questions" instead of guessing.
- Plain, direct language. Lead with the decision, not the background.
- Keep it to one page. A reader should grasp the call in the first three lines.

The decision and context:
[PASTE THE PENDING CALL + WHAT YOU KNOW — the question, the options, what you've heard, constraints, deadline, who's affected. Bullets are fine.]
```

## Before and after

**Before — the scattered version in your head:**
> We probably can't keep building both the API and the dashboard with this team. Sarah thinks API, Marco wants dashboard. Two big customers asked for the API. Dashboard demos better in sales. We're stretched thin. Need to decide before planning next week.

**After — the kind of memo it produces:**

> **The decision.** We'll focus the team on the API for the next quarter and pause new dashboard work.
>
> **Why**
> - Two of our largest customers have asked for the API directly. The dashboard demand is softer.
> - The team can't ship both well right now, and splitting it has slowed both.
> - The API unblocks integrations that the dashboard later depends on anyway.
>
> **Options considered**
> - *Keep building both.* Why not: it's what we're doing, and neither is shipping.
> - *Focus on the dashboard.* Why not: it demos well but lacks the committed customer pull the API has.
>
> **Risks and what would change my mind**
> - Sales may lose dashboard momentum. I'd reconsider if two or more deals stall on it this quarter.
> - If a third customer drops the API ask, the case weakens.
>
> **What happens next**
> - Sarah leads API delivery; she'll bring a scope and timeline to next week's planning.
> - Marco documents the dashboard state so we can restart cleanly later.
>
> **Open questions**
> - No deadline was given for the customer API commitments — confirm before we lock scope.

## Make it yours

- **Set the audience.** Add *"This goes to my exec team"* or *"This is for the board"* — the framing and detail shift.
- **Stress-test it first.** Add *"Before the memo, argue the strongest case against my decision."* If the case holds up, the memo is better for it.
- **Match how your team writes.** Paste an old memo and add *"Use this structure and tone."*
- **Trim or expand.** *"Make this half as long"* for a quick call, or *"add a one-paragraph summary at the top"* for a heavier one.

## Watch out for

- **The format is not the decision.** A clean memo can make a half-baked call look finished. The structure is the tool's job; the judgment is yours. A tidy layout is not evidence that the reasoning is sound.
- **Check that the recommendation is what you actually believe.** AI will write a confident case for whatever you feed it — including the wrong call. Read the "why" and the chosen option, and make sure they match your real view, not just the cleanest-sounding one.
- **Own the reasoning, not just the words.** You'll defend this memo in the room. If a point in it isn't one you'd stand behind out loud, cut it or rewrite it before you send.
- **Mind the gaps it papers over.** If the tool guessed an owner, a date, or a fact you didn't give it, catch it. Move anything uncertain to "Open questions" rather than letting a placeholder read as settled.

pages.title: Ask Better Questions of the Numbers
pages.description: Get a sharper set of questions to put to a finance report before a review.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've got a finance report in front of you and a review coming up. The numbers are there. What's missing is the right set of questions to put to them. Without them, you react to the charts in the meeting instead of testing what's behind them.

Use this when you want a sharper line of questioning before the meeting. The model reads your figures and hands back the pointed questions worth asking. It doesn't answer them. You decide which ones to raise, and what the answers mean.

## The prompt

```
You are helping an executive prepare for a finance review. I'll paste a report or a set of figures below. Your job is to give me a sharp list of questions to ask about it — not answers, not your own analysis of the numbers.

Return:
1. The questions I should put to whoever owns this report, grouped by theme (e.g. revenue, costs, cash, assumptions).
2. For each question, one line on why it's worth asking — what it would surface.
3. A short list of anything that looks missing or that I'd need before I could read this confidently.

Rules:
- Ask questions. Do not state conclusions about the numbers or compute new figures.
- Do not restate my figures back as if they're verified facts. If you reference one, mark it as "you reported X" so it's clear it came from me.
- Plain language. No jargon. Each question should be one a smart non-finance person could ask out loud.

Here's the report:
[PASTE THE REPORT OR FIGURES — tables, a summary, raw notes. Whatever you have.]

The review is about: [WHAT THIS MEETING IS FOR — e.g. quarterly close, a spend approval, a board prep].
What I most want to understand: [THE ONE OR TWO THINGS ON YOUR MIND].
```

## Before and after

**Before — the figures you'd paste in:**
> Q3 revenue $4.2M, up from $3.8M in Q2. Gross margin 61%, was 64% last Q. Sales headcount up 30%. Cash burn $600K/mo, runway 11 months. New enterprise tier launched mid-Q. <!-- noev: worked example -->

**After — the kind of questions it gives back:**

> **Revenue**
> - You reported revenue up to $4.2M. How much of that growth came from the new enterprise tier versus the existing base? It tells you if the new bet is working or just masking flat core sales. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Is any of the $4.2M one-time or pulled forward from Q4? It separates a real trend from a good-looking quarter. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **Margin and costs**
> - Margin dropped from 64% to 61%. What drove it — the new tier's cost to serve, or something else? It tells you whether the dip is a choice or a leak. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Sales headcount is up 30%. Is revenue per rep holding, or did we add people faster than they're producing? It surfaces whether the hiring is paying off yet. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **Cash**
> - At $600K/month burn, what's the single biggest assumption behind the 11-month runway figure? It shows you how fragile the timeline is. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **Looks missing**
> - No customer count or churn, so you can't tell if revenue grew from more customers or higher prices.
> - No prior-year comparison — only quarter-over-quarter. Seasonality could be hiding here.

## Make it yours

- **Aim it at one decision.** Add *"Only give me questions that matter for approving next quarter's spend."*
- **Set the depth.** *"Give me the five sharpest questions, not twenty"* — or ask for an exhaustive list if you're going deep.
- **Stress-test the rosy read.** Add *"Assume this report is putting its best foot forward. What would a skeptical board member ask?"*
- **Prep the follow-up.** *"For each question, give me a likely answer and the follow-up I'd ask if it's evasive."*

## Watch out for

- **The model probes; you verify.** Its job is to ask good questions, not to vouch for your figures. Don't let it restate or recompute a number — it can copy one wrong or quietly do bad math. Every figure you carry into the room gets checked against the source first.
- **A confident question isn't a confirmed problem.** A sharp question can imply the number is wrong when it's fine. The question is a prompt to look — the answer comes from the report and the people who own it, not the model.
- **You own which questions to ask.** A list of twenty questions isn't a plan. Pick the few that fit the room and the decision. Reading the answers is your call, not the tool's.

pages.title: Prep for a Budget Review
pages.description: Walk into a budget review knowing the trade-offs and the calls you need to make.
pages.body:
## When to use this

A budget review forces trade-offs. Someone wants more headcount. Someone wants a cut reversed. You decide what gets funded and what waits.

Use this before you walk in. The goal is to know the trade-offs cold, so the calls you make are yours.

This isn't a single prompt. It's a back-and-forth. You feed the AI the numbers, it surfaces the tensions, and you work toward the asks you'll defend.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop with something like this, then keep going turn by turn:

```
You're helping me prepare for a budget review. I'll paste my budget below.

Don't just summarize it. Find the trade-offs. Where are two things competing for the same money? Where is spend growing faster than the work behind it? Where would a cut hurt least, and where would it hurt most?

List the 6–8 sharpest trade-offs first, then wait. I'll work through them with you one at a time.

My budget:
[PASTE YOUR BUDGET OR THE KEY LINES]
```

## Work through it step by step

1. **Surface the trade-offs first.** Read the list before you react to any one item. The tensions you didn't see coming are where this prep pays off.
2. **Find what you'd cut, and what it costs.** Pick a line and ask: if I cut this, what breaks? Push for the second-order effect, not just the saving. A cheap cut with a hidden cost is the one that bites later.
3. **Pressure-test each ask.** For every increase you want, have it play the skeptic. Make it ask why now, why this much, why not later. If your reason wobbles, fix it here, not in the room.
4. **Prep your answers.** Walk through the questions you'll get on the hard lines. Say the answer out loud, then refine it until it holds up.
5. **Decide your asks.** Now name what you're actually walking in to defend. The list is yours: what you'll fund, what you'll cut, what you'll fight for.

## What stays your call

The allocations are yours. Full stop. The AI can lay out the trade-offs and press on your reasoning. It can't decide what your business needs next quarter.

It doesn't know which cut you can live with, or which bet is worth the risk. Use it to see the trade-offs clearly. Then make the calls yourself, and own them.

## Watch out for

- **Never trust it to restate a number.** AI will misread or miscopy a figure and say it with total confidence. Check every number it repeats against your source before you carry it in.
- **The trade-offs are a map, not a verdict.** It will rank what to cut as if it knows your priorities. It doesn't. The ranking is your decision, not its suggestion.
- **Keep sensitive lines out of a tool you don't control.** Unannounced cuts, specific salaries, anything confidential — work those through offline or in general terms.

pages.title: Turn a Finance Deck into a One-Page Read
pages.description: Compress a long finance deck into a one-page read you can act on.
pages.body:
## When to use this

Someone's sent you a 40-slide finance deck. You have ten minutes before the meeting. You don't need every chart — you need the gist. What changed, what's good, what's bad, and what calls for a decision.

Use this when you want a fast, plain summary you can act on. Paste in the deck text or your notes, and get back one page. You stay in charge of the figures and the call.

## The prompt

```
You are helping an executive read a finance deck fast. I'll paste the deck's text and notes below. Turn it into a one-page read.

Structure it as:
1. Headline — two or three sentences. How did the period go overall? Lead with the single thing that matters most.
2. What changed — the key movements since last period, each with the number. Up, down, or flat. Keep it short.
3. What needs a decision — the one to three items that call for a choice or a follow-up. Say what the choice is.

Rules:
- Use only the numbers and facts I give you. Do not invent or estimate any figure.
- If a number looks important but is missing, list it under "Gaps" instead of guessing.
- Plain language. No jargon, no hype. Short sentences.
- Keep the whole thing to one page.

Deck text and notes:
[PASTE THE DECK TEXT OR YOUR NOTES — revenue, costs, margin, cash, variances, anything flagged. Rough is fine.]
```

## Before and after

**Before — the dense notes you'd paste in:**

> - Q3 rev $4.2M vs $3.8M Q2, plan was $4.5M <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Gross margin 61%, down from 64% last Q, cloud costs up <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Opex flat ~$2.1M <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Cash $6.8M, burn $700K/mo, ~9 mo runway <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - One vendor contract renews next month, 18% price hike proposed <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Sales hiring behind, 3 of 5 roles open

**After — the one-pager it produces:**

> **Headline.** Revenue grew to $4.2M but landed under the $4.5M plan. Margin slipped on rising cloud costs, and runway is tight at about nine months. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **What changed**
> - Revenue up to $4.2M from $3.8M last quarter — short of the $4.5M plan. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Gross margin fell to 61% from 64%, driven by higher cloud costs. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Operating expenses held flat at about $2.1M. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Cash at $6.8M, burning $700K a month — roughly nine months of runway. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **What needs a decision**
> - A vendor wants an 18% price increase at next month's renewal. Accept, negotiate, or switch. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Sales hiring is behind, with three of five roles open. Decide whether to push harder or hold. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **Gaps**
> - No customer or retention numbers in the notes — add them if they drove the miss.

## Make it yours

- **Change the lens.** Add *"Read this as a cash question — lead with runway and burn."*
- **Match your format.** Paste last month's one-pager and add *"Use this same shape and order."*
- **Get the questions, not just the summary.** Add *"List the three sharpest questions I should ask in the meeting."* (See [Ask Better Questions of the Numbers](/ai-use-cases/executive-leadership/finance-performance/ask-better-questions-of-the-numbers.md).)
- **Tighten it more.** Add *"Cut it to five bullets I could read aloud in a minute."*

## Watch out for

- **Check every figure against the deck.** This is the one that bites. A summary can drop a number, round it wrong, or swap two of them — and the clean layout makes the error look trustworthy. Before you rely on a figure or repeat it, find it in the source deck and confirm it matches.
- **A confident summary can still distort.** The tool can frame a flat quarter as good news, or bury a real risk in a tidy bullet. Read the deck's flagged items yourself and make sure each one survived.
- **You own the decision, not the tool.** The one-pager tells you what changed. What to do about the price hike or the hiring gap is your call — and your finance team's input — not the summary's.

pages.title: Draft and Sharpen Performance Feedback
pages.description: Turn rough notes into clear, specific, fair performance feedback.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've watched someone's work for a quarter, and now you owe them feedback. You know what you want to say. But your notes are a jumble — a few wins, a couple of worries, some half-formed phrases. The judgment is done. The hard part is saying it clearly, specifically, and fairly.

Use this when you've decided what the feedback is, and you want a clean draft to sharpen. It turns rough notes into structured, balanced wording you then edit and make your own.

## The prompt

```
You are helping a manager write clear, specific, fair performance feedback. Turn the rough notes below into structured feedback I can edit.

Structure it as:
1. Summary — two or three sentences on the overall picture.
2. Strengths — what's going well, each tied to a specific behavior or example.
3. Areas to grow — what needs to change, stated plainly and kindly, each tied to a specific behavior.
4. Suggested next steps — a few concrete things this person could do.

Rules:
- Use only what I tell you. Do not invent examples, numbers, or context. If a point is vague, leave it general rather than inventing detail.
- Be specific. Tie each point to a behavior, not a personality trait. "Missed two deadlines" — not "is disorganized."
- Be balanced and fair. Don't pad the strengths or soften the growth areas into nothing.
- Plain, warm, direct language. Write it so I could say it out loud in a review.

My rough notes:
[PASTE YOUR NOTES — wins, concerns, specific moments, anything you want to land. Describe the behavior, not the person. Bullet points are fine.]
```

## Before and after

**Before — the kind of notes you'd start with:**
> - really strong on the client work, the QBR deck was great
> - drops the ball on follow-up, things fall through the cracks
> - quiet in meetings, has good ideas but doesn't share them
> - ready for more ownership I think

**After — the kind of feedback it produces:**

> **Summary.** A strong quarter on client-facing work, with clear readiness for more ownership. The main area to grow is follow-through, so good work doesn't stall after the first push.
>
> **Strengths**
> - Excellent client work — the QBR deck stood out, and clients noticed.
> - Brings good ideas to the team and a high bar for quality.
>
> **Areas to grow**
> - Follow-up is inconsistent; some items slip after the initial work is done.
> - Tends to stay quiet in meetings, so strong ideas don't always reach the room.
>
> **Suggested next steps**
> - Try a simple system to track open items to close.
> - Share one idea per meeting, even a rough one.

Same substance as the notes, now specific, balanced, and ready to edit.

## Make it yours

- **Set the tone.** Add *"This person is doing well and I want it to feel encouraging"* — or *"This is a serious conversation; keep it direct."*
- **Match your review format.** Paste your company's template and add *"Use this structure."*
- **Get specific phrasing help.** *"Suggest three ways to word the follow-through point — gentlest to most direct."*
- **Pressure-test fairness.** Add *"Flag anywhere this reads as a comment on the person rather than the work."*

## Watch out for

- **Don't paste in a real person's identity.** Describe the behavior, not the name. Don't put an employee's name, title, or identifying detail into a chat tool you don't control. Write *"a manager on my team"* and the actions, not who they are. The wording is what you need help with, not the file.
- **Don't let the draft soften what you mean to land.** AI tends to round the edges off hard feedback. If a message needs to be clear and firm, read the draft and make sure it still says what you decided to say. Keep the substance honest — kind wording, but no message lost.

pages.title: Prep a Hard 1:1 or Personnel Conversation
pages.description: Rehearse and structure a difficult personnel conversation before you have it.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You have a hard conversation coming. A performance issue, a role change, a hard no. You know roughly what you want to say. You also know it could go sideways fast.

Use this to rehearse before the real thing. You play yourself; the AI plays the other person, or coaches you. You try an opening, hear how it lands, and try again. Running it a few times helps more than getting one line perfect.

This is a back-and-forth. You say a line, the AI reacts, you adjust. Run it a few rounds. The goal is to walk in calmer and clearer, with the hard turns already rehearsed.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop with something like this, then keep going round after round:

```
I have a difficult conversation to prepare for. I'll keep names and identifying details out of this — treat the other person in general terms.

The situation: [ONE OR TWO SENTENCES, NO NAMES]
My goal for the conversation: [WHAT YOU WANT TO BE TRUE WHEN IT ENDS]
What I'm worried about: [THE REACTION OR TURN YOU DREAD]

Play the other person. Stay in character and react the way someone in that spot plausibly would. I'll give you my opening line. Push back, get defensive, or go quiet — whatever fits. After each round, drop character briefly and tell me what landed and what didn't.

Here's my opening:
[YOUR FIRST LINE]
```

You can also flip it. Ask the AI to coach instead of role-play: "Don't play them — critique my line and suggest a clearer one."

## Work through it step by step

1. **Name your goal first.** Before any wording, get clear on what you want true when it ends. Clarity for them? A behavior change? A clean exit? The goal shapes every line after it.
2. **Draft the opening out loud.** Say your first two sentences as you'd really say them, then type them in. The opening sets the tone for the whole conversation. Get it human, not robotic.
3. **Role-play the reactions.** Let the AI push back as the other person. They get defensive; you respond. They go quiet; you respond. Run the turns that scare you most.
4. **Tighten what didn't land.** When a line comes out cold or vague, fix it. Ask: "Say that more directly but still kind." Keep the version that sounds like you.
5. **Plan for the hard turn.** There's usually one moment that could derail it — tears, anger, a "this isn't fair." Rehearse that exact moment. Have a calm next line ready so you're not improvising under pressure.
6. **Do one last clean run.** Walk the whole conversation start to finish. If it still sounds like a script, loosen it until it sounds like you talking.

## What stays your call

The relationship, the empathy, and the decision are all yours. The AI can suggest words and how they might land. But it doesn't know the person you're talking to, and it isn't the one making the call.

A rehearsed line should still sound like you. If the AI hands you something polished but not your voice, change it. The other person will know the difference between a real conversation and a recited one. Use the prep to be ready, not to be scripted.

## Watch out for

- **Keep it anonymous.** Don't paste a real person's name, role, or identifying details into a chat tool you don't control. Rehearse in general terms — "a team member," not who. The situation matters for the prep; their identity doesn't.
- **Don't rehearse away the empathy.** It's easy to optimize a hard message into something efficient and cold. Stay warm. The point is to deliver hard news like a person, not to win the exchange.
- **A smooth script can sound fake.** If you memorize lines word for word, you'll sound like you're reading. Rehearse the shape and the hard turns, then trust yourself to talk in the room.
- **The AI's version of the other person is a guess.** It doesn't know this person. Use the role-play to practice staying steady, not to predict exactly what they'll say.

pages.title: Think Through an Org or Hiring Decision
pages.description: Lay out the options and trade-offs behind an org-design or senior-hire decision.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You're sitting on an org or hiring call that won't settle. Maybe you're weighing whether to split a team in two. Maybe it's a senior hire, and two finalists pull you in different directions. The shape of the right answer is fuzzy, and you keep circling.

Used well, the AI widens the field instead of handing you one answer. You frame the question, it lays out the options, and you stress-test each one. Your leaning gets tested before you commit, so you find the trade-off you'd otherwise miss.

Work through it turn by turn, not in one prompt.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop with something like this, then keep going turn by turn:

```
I'm working through an org/hiring decision and want you to think it through with me, not just agree.

The situation: [DESCRIBE THE DECISION IN GENERAL TERMS — NO REAL NAMES OR PERSONNEL DETAIL].
What I'm trying to achieve: [THE OUTCOME THAT MATTERS MOST].
The constraints I'm working under: [BUDGET, TIMING, WHAT I CAN'T CHANGE].

Don't jump to a recommendation. First, play back what you think the real question is — the decision under the decision. Then wait for me to confirm or correct it.
```

## Work through it step by step

1. **Frame the real question.** Let it play back the decision under the decision. Often the question you typed isn't the one that matters. Correct it until the framing feels true, then move on.
2. **Lay out the options.** Ask for three or four distinct paths, not two. Push it to include the option you've been avoiding. A real choice needs more than yes-or-no.
3. **Stress each one.** Take the options one at a time. For each, ask: who does this help, who does it cost, and what breaks if it goes wrong? Make it argue the case against the option it just described.
4. **Surface the second-order effects.** Ask what each path does six months out. Org calls ripple — to morale, to who you can hire next, to what signal it sends. Get those on the table now.
5. **Pressure-test your leaning.** Say which way you're leaning and why. Then ask it to make the strongest case that you're wrong. If your reasoning survives, act on it. If it doesn't, better to learn that now than after you commit.

## What stays your call

The decision is yours, full stop. So is the read on the people involved. The AI can map options and poke holes in them. It can't feel whether someone's ready to step up, or how a team will actually take the news. It has no stake in the outcome and no relationship with anyone in the room. Use it to widen your thinking and test your logic. Then make the call the way only someone who knows these people can.

## Watch out for

- **Keep real people out of a tool you don't control.** Don't paste in employee names, performance details, or anything that identifies a person. Reason in general terms — "a senior IC who's ready for more" works fine, and keeps sensitive personnel detail off a system you don't own.
- **It has no context on the humans.** The AI will weigh options cleanly because it doesn't know anyone. That's useful for logic and dangerous for people. The thing it can't see — how this lands for the actual person — is usually the thing that matters most.
- **A confident option list isn't a recommendation.** It'll lay out paths persuasively, including ones that don't fit your reality. Treat the options as raw material for your judgment, not a verdict. Drop the ones that don't apply; add the one it missed.

pages.title: Pressure-Test a Plan with Scenarios
pages.description: Stress a plan against best-, base-, and worst-case scenarios before you commit.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've got a plan you like. The team's behind it. You're close to committing real money and time. That's the moment to stress it.

Use this before you sign off on a bet. A new market, a big hire, a product line, a price change. Anything where being wrong is expensive.

This isn't one prompt. It's a loop. You make the AI argue against you, turn after turn. It builds the scenarios, pokes the soft spots, and tries to break your plan. You hold the line on what matters and decide what to fix.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop like this. Then keep going.

```
You're a sharp, skeptical strategist. I'm about to commit to a plan, and I
want you to stress-test it before I do. Be a real devil's advocate. Don't
flatter it.

Here's the plan:
[DESCRIBE THE PLAN — the bet, the timeline, the resources]

Here's what I'm assuming is true:
[LIST YOUR KEY ASSUMPTIONS — demand, cost, timing, who does what]

First, build three scenarios for how this plays out: best case, base case,
worst case. Be concrete about what's different in each. Then wait.

After that, I'll ask you to dig into specific parts. Keep pushing. When I
give you a soft answer, say so.
```

## Work through it step by step

1. **Build the three scenarios.** Read all three before you react. The best case is easy; the worst case is where you learn something, so don't skim past it.
2. **Probe your assumptions.** Pick the assumptions the plan rests on. Ask the AI to challenge each one. "What if demand is half what I think?" Make it argue the other side hard.
3. **Find what breaks the plan.** Ask straight out: what single thing, if it went wrong, sinks this? There's usually one or two — finding them is the point of the exercise.
4. **Pressure the worst case.** Don't let it stay vague. Ask how you'd see it coming. Ask what the early warning sign would be. Ask what you'd do the day it happens.
5. **Decide what to harden.** Now you choose. Some risks you accept and move on. Some you fix before you commit. Some you set a tripwire for. Write down which is which.
6. **Run it past someone real.** The AI found the questions. A trusted colleague tells you if the fix holds. Close the loop with a human before you sign.

## What stays your call

The bet is yours. So is the line between a risk worth taking and one that isn't.

The AI is great at building scenarios and pushing on weak spots. It has no stake in the outcome. It doesn't know your appetite for risk, your timing, or what your gut is telling you.

Use it to surface what could go wrong. Then you decide what you can live with. Never let a tidy scenario talk you out of a bet you believe in, or into one you don't.

## Watch out for

- **A confident scenario isn't a forecast.** The AI will lay out a clean best, base, and worst case. They're prompts for your thinking, not predictions. The real worst case might be one it never named.
- **Don't let it round off the messy parts.** It tends to make plans look neater than they are. The friction, the politics, the thing nobody wants to say out loud — those are often what breaks a plan. Add them back.
- **Keep deal specifics out of a tool you don't control.** Named people, unannounced moves, anything sensitive — stress-test those in general terms, or offline.
- **Don't over-fix.** You can't harden against everything. If you try, you'll never commit. Pick the few risks that matter and let the rest go.

pages.title: Run a Strategy-Review Prep
pages.description: Pull scattered inputs into a tight pre-read so the strategy review starts on the real questions.
pages.body:
## When to use this

A strategy review is coming up. The inputs are everywhere. Notes from three meetings, a half-finished deck, last quarter's numbers, two conflicting memos. Use this to pull all of it into one tight pre-read. The point isn't a prettier document. It's starting the review on the real questions, not the warm-up.

This isn't a one-shot prompt. It's a loop. You feed in the raw material, the AI drafts a pre-read, and then you push on it. You cut what doesn't matter, sharpen what does, and force the hard questions to the front. Treat the AI as a thinking partner that organizes, not one that decides.

## A prompt to start

Open the loop with something like this, then keep going turn by turn:

```
You are helping me prep a pre-read for an upcoming strategy review. I'll paste in my scattered inputs below — notes, numbers, memos, half-formed thoughts.

First, organize all of it into a draft pre-read: where we are, what's changed since last time, and the open decisions on the table. Keep it tight. Flag anything that contradicts itself or looks thin.

Then list the 5–7 questions this review should actually start on — the real ones, not the easy ones. Rank them. Then wait.
```
