```
I'll react, cut, and reorder. We'll go back and forth until the pre-read is sharp.

My inputs:
[PASTE YOUR NOTES, NUMBERS, AND MEMOS]
[WHAT THIS REVIEW NEEDS TO DECIDE]
```

## Work through it step by step

1. **Dump everything in first.** Don't tidy it up for the AI. Messy notes, conflicting figures, loose ends — paste it all. The organizing is its job, and the contradictions are exactly what you want surfaced.
2. **Read the draft for what's missing.** It will arrange what you gave it. It can't know what you left out. Scan for the input that should be there and isn't, then add it and ask for another pass.
3. **Push on the question list.** This is the heart of it. Ask why those questions and not others. Cut the ones that are really last quarter's debate. Add the one everybody's avoiding.
4. **Make it cut, not pad.** A good pre-read is short. If a section is filler, tell it to drop it. Keep pressing: "What can come out and still leave the decision clear?"
5. **Pressure-test the framing.** Ask it to argue the opposite of how you've framed a decision. If the reframing is stronger, you found that before the room did.
6. **End on the opening question.** Settle what the review starts on. One sharp question beats a status recap. That single line is the real output of the session.

## What stays your call

The questions are yours. So is what makes the cut. The AI is good at organizing scattered inputs and proposing what to ask. It has no idea which decision actually matters most to your business, or which question your team needs to face this quarter. Use it to pull the pre-read together and to surface candidates. Then you decide what the review opens on, and what gets left out. Don't let a tidy draft talk you past the question you know is the one that counts.

## Watch out for

- **Tidy is not the same as right.** A clean pre-read can read well and still bury the real issue. Organized output feels finished. Check that the hard decision is on the page, not smoothed over.
- **Keep sensitive specifics out of a tool you don't control.** Unannounced deals, named personnel issues, legal exposure — don't paste those in. Prep those parts in general terms, or offline.
- **It can flatten a real disagreement into a neat summary.** If two memos conflict, that tension might be the whole point. Make sure the pre-read shows the disagreement instead of papering over it.
- **The question list is a starting point, not a script.** It will propose questions that don't fit your situation. You know the review. Drop what doesn't apply and add what it missed.

pages.title: Scan a Market or Competitor Fast
pages.description: Turn raw notes and links into a quick read on a market or competitor.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've been gathering scraps about a market or a rival. A few links, some notes from a call, a screenshot of their pricing page. It's a pile, not a picture. You need a fast first read before a meeting, a board prep, or a gut check on a new bet.

Use this when you want that pile turned into a clean, structured scan you can react to. The tool does the sorting. You bring the judgment — what's reliable, and what it all means.

## The prompt

```
You are helping an executive get a fast first read on a market or competitor. Turn the raw material below into a structured scan.

Structure it as:
1. Snapshot — three or four sentences. What is this market or company, and why does it matter to me right now?
2. What stands out — the three or four most notable points from the material I gave you.
3. Strengths and weaknesses — a short two-column read, based only on what's in the notes.
4. Open questions — what I'd need to find out before I could act on this.
5. Confidence flags — mark any claim you're unsure about, or that looks like it might be out of date.

Rules:
- Use mainly the material I provide. If you add general context from your own knowledge, label it clearly as "[general context — verify]" so I know to check it.
- Do not invent numbers, dates, market sizes, or funding figures. If a key fact is missing, say so under "Open questions" instead of guessing.
- Flag anything that may have changed since your training — pricing, headcount, leadership, funding.
- Plain, direct language. No hype.

Material:
[PASTE YOUR RAW NOTES AND LINKS — call notes, pricing, product pages, articles, your own observations. Mark anything you already know is dated.]
```

## Before and after

**Before — the pile you'd paste in:**

> - Competitor: Northwind, sells scheduling software to clinics <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Their pricing page: $49/user/month, was $39 last I checked <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Heard on a call they just raised a Series B, maybe $30M? not sure <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Customers love the calendar, complain support is slow
> - We're cheaper but our onboarding is clunky
> - Article said the clinic-software market is "growing fast"

**After — the kind of scan it produces:**

> **Snapshot.** Northwind is a scheduling tool for clinics, positioned a tier above us on price. It matters now because they appear to have fresh funding and are pushing upmarket, into our space. The read below is based on your notes; several figures need verifying before you act. <!-- noev: worked example -->
>
> **What stands out**
> - Priced at $49/user/month — above us, and reportedly up from $39, which may signal confidence or pressure. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - A possible Series B (~$30M, unconfirmed) would give them runway to outspend us on growth. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Their calendar is a loved feature; their support is a known weak spot.
>
> **Strengths and weaknesses**
>
> | Strengths | Weaknesses |
> |---|---|
> | Strong core calendar | Slow support |
> | Apparent new funding | Higher price than you |
>
> **Open questions**
> - Is the ~$30M Series B real, and when did it close? <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - Is $49 the live price, or a plan tier? Confirm on their site. <!-- noev: worked example -->
> - How big is the clinic-scheduling market, really? "Growing fast" isn't a number.
>
> **Confidence flags**
> - Funding amount and round: unconfirmed, from a call.
> - Pricing: may be out of date — verify directly. <!-- noev: worked example -->

## Make it yours

- **Frame it against you.** Add *"Compare each point to my company, whose pitch is [PASTE]."*
- **Aim it at a decision.** Add *"I'm deciding whether to enter this market — sharpen the scan toward that call."*
- **Ask for the bear case.** Add *"End with the strongest reason this competitor beats us."*
- **Set the depth.** *"Keep it to half a page"* for a gut check, or *"go deep on their pricing"* when that's the crux.
- **Hand it a transcript.** Paste a raw call or interview and add *"Pull the market signals out of this."*

## Watch out for

- **Treat every fact as unverified until you check it.** The model may not have current data, and it can state stale or made-up facts with total confidence. A funding round, a price, a headcount, a market size — any of it can be wrong. Verify anything that drives a decision against the live source.
- **A scan is a starting point, not the verdict.** The structure makes a messy pile readable. It does not make the underlying facts true. The strategic conclusion is yours to draw.
- **Watch for confident filler.** If you gave it thin material, it may pad with plausible-sounding generalities. Push back: *"Only use what I gave you."* Then judge what's actually solid.

pages.title: How AI Helps Realtors
pages.description: How this bundle groups AI prompt recipes for agents, and where an agent must keep human judgment.
pages.body:
Most of an agent's day is words: a listing that has to sing, a follow-up that can't sound
canned, a script for a nervous seller, a plain answer to "what's a contingency?" AI is good
at first drafts of exactly this. These recipes hand you the prompt and show you the result,
so you spend your time editing instead of staring at a blank screen.

## How the recipes are grouped

Each recipe lives under the job you're doing, not the tool you're using:

- **[Listings & Marketing](/ai-use-cases/realtors/listings-and-marketing/index.md)** — the copy that sells a property and fills an open house.
- **[Prospecting & Outreach](/ai-use-cases/realtors/prospecting-and-outreach/index.md)** — the messages that start conversations with new sellers and your own sphere.
- **[Client Communication](/ai-use-cases/realtors/client-communication/index.md)** — the notes that keep a client informed and comfortable through a deal.

Two more groups are on the way as recipes are written: **Market & Advising** (neighborhood
and pricing explainers) and **Transactions & Admin** (showing notes, checklists, disclosure
explainers). A group shows up here once it has its first recipe.

## What every recipe looks like

The shape is the same each time, so you learn it once:

- **When to use this** — the moment the recipe fits.
- **The prompt** — paste-ready, with the blanks you fill in.
- **Before and after** — the raw input and what the AI turned it into.
- **Make it yours** — how to tune the voice and reuse it.
- **Watch out for** — only where a recipe touches a rule you can't get wrong.

## Where you keep the judgment

This is the part AI does not do for you. An agent stays on the hook for three things, every
time:

- **Fair housing.** AI will happily write phrases that steer or describe the *buyer* instead
  of the home. That's a legal risk. You read every listing and every ad with fair-housing
  eyes before it goes out.
- **Accuracy.** Square footage, school ratings, "walk to the train," what a clause means —
  the AI guesses when it doesn't know. You confirm the facts.
- **It isn't legal advice.** A plain-language explainer helps a client understand a term. It
  doesn't replace their attorney, and you shouldn't let it read that way.

The recipes flag these where they bite. But the rule holds even where a page stays quiet:
the AI drafts, and you own what you send.

pages.title: Ask for a Review
pages.description: Ask a happy client for a review after closing.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You just closed a deal and your client is thrilled. That's the moment to ask — not weeks later when the feeling has worn off. But a generic "please leave me a review" rarely lands well. Use this to write a short, warm message that's easy to send and easy for them to act on.

It works for any closing: buyer, seller, rental, or referral.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets.

```text
You're helping me ask a satisfied client for an online review after closing.
Write a short, warm, personal message I can send by text or email.

Details:
- Client name: [first name or first names]
- One highlight of the deal: [e.g. "we found them a home in a tough market,"
  "I helped them sell above asking," "we closed in 21 days"]
- Where I'd like the review: [Google / Zillow / both]
- Tone: [friendly and warm / professional / casual]

Rules:
- Open with something personal — reference the highlight, not a generic
  "it was great working with you."
- Keep it under 80 words.
- Include ONE clear call to action with a placeholder for the direct review
  link: [YOUR GOOGLE REVIEW LINK] or [YOUR ZILLOW REVIEW LINK].
- Do NOT offer or imply any reward, discount, gift, or incentive for leaving
  a review.
- Do NOT ask them to only leave a positive review — the ask must be open
  to honest feedback.
- End on a warm, low-pressure note. No guilt, no urgency.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your rough idea before the prompt:

> Hey! If you have a second, would you mind leaving me a Google review? It would really help me out. Thanks so much!

**What you get back:**

> Hi Sarah and James — finding you that corner unit in a market this tight was genuinely one of my favorite moments this year. If you're up for it, I'd love it if you shared your honest experience: [YOUR GOOGLE REVIEW LINK]. No pressure at all — I just wanted you to know how much this one meant to me.

The rewrite names a moment they'll actually remember, makes the ask feel personal, and leaves zero pressure.

## Make it yours

- **Send it while it's fresh.** The day of closing or the day after works best. Waiting a week cuts response rates in half.
- **Text over email.** A short text gets read and acted on faster. Save email for clients who prefer it.
- **Make the link one tap.** Go to your Google Business Profile or Zillow agent page, copy the direct review URL, and paste it in before you send. A link that takes two steps loses people.
- **One platform, not both.** Asking for reviews on two platforms in the same message feels like homework. Pick the one that matters most to your business right now.
- **Customize the highlight.** Swap in the exact detail that made this deal memorable. The more specific it is, the more it sounds like you.

## Watch out for

**Don't offer anything in exchange.** The FTC's Rule on Consumer Reviews and Testimonials (effective October 2024) prohibits offering payment, discounts, gifts, or any incentive in exchange for a review. Google's policy says the same: no "payment, discounts, free goods and/or services in exchange for posting any review." The prompt enforces this, but double-check any edits you make before you send.

**Don't screen clients before you ask.** Review gating means sending the ask only to clients you expect to be happy, while skipping the ones who had a rough experience. Google explicitly prohibits it: "discouraging or prohibiting negative reviews, or selectively soliciting positive reviews from customers." Ask everyone, or set a neutral policy — for example, every closed transaction. The prompt keeps the ask open to honest feedback, which is the right posture.

**Reviews must be genuine.** Both the FTC and Google require reviews to reflect real, firsthand experience. Don't coach clients on what to say, ask them to mention a specific detail, or suggest a rating. Let them write what they actually experienced.

pages.title: Buyer and Seller Follow-Ups
pages.description: Draft follow-up messages after a showing or an inquiry.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You just finished a showing or answered an inquiry. The client left happy, or at least interested, and now you need to follow up before they move on. Use this to get a warm, professional message drafted fast — fill in the brackets, paste, done.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets. Leave a line blank if it doesn't apply.

```text
You're helping me write a real estate follow-up message to a client.
Draft a short, warm message based on the details below.

Context:
- Type of contact: [showing / inquiry / open house]
- Client name: [first name, or "the Garcias"]
- Property: [address or short description — e.g. "the 3-bed on Elm"]
- What happened: [what they saw, what they asked about, what stood out]
- Any specifics to mention: [a question they had, a feature they loved, a next step]
- Tone: [warm / professional / low-pressure]
- My name: [your name]

Rules:
- Keep it under 100 words.
- Sound like a person, not a template.
- Do NOT make promises or commitments I didn't approve — no "I'll get you an offer by Friday,"
  no "the sellers will definitely negotiate."
- End with one clear, easy next step (a reply, a call, a second showing).
- Use ONLY the facts I gave you. Do not invent details about the property.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your notes after the showing:

> Showed the Garcias the Maple Ave place this afternoon. They loved the backyard and the open kitchen. Mom seemed really into the primary suite. Dad asked about the HOA fees — I said I'd look it up. They seemed warm but not in a rush. Want to stay top of mind.

**What you get back:**

> Hi Maria and Carlos,
>
> It was great walking through the Maple Avenue home with you today. That backyard and open kitchen are hard to beat — and the primary suite really does deliver. I'm looking up the HOA details for you now and will send them over shortly.
>
> No pressure at all — just want to make sure you have everything you need to think it over. Happy to schedule a second look whenever you're ready. Just reply here or give me a call.
>
> Talk soon,
> Jamie

The HOA answer is pending — and the message says so without overpromising.

## Make it yours

- **Adjust the tone.** "Low-pressure" works well for early-stage buyers. Change it to "professional" for a seller following up after an offer, or "warm" for a long-term client you know well.
- **Add the next step explicitly.** If you want them to book a second showing, say so in the specifics field. The closing line will reflect it.
- **Use it for inquiries too.** Someone emailed about a listing they haven't seen yet? Fill in the context as "inquiry" and describe what they asked. Same prompt.
- **The AI drafts — you own what you promise.** Read every message before you send it. If the draft makes a commitment you're not ready to keep, edit it out. The prompt tells it not to promise, but read before you send.

pages.title: Explain an Offer or Contingency
pages.description: Explain an offer term or a contingency to a client in plain language.
pages.body:
## When to use this

Your client just read the offer and their eyes glazed over. The inspection contingency, the financing clause, the earnest money terms — it's dense, and they're nervous. Use this to turn any contract term into a short, clear explanation they can actually act on. It works whether you're reviewing an offer your buyer is about to sign or walking a seller through what the buyer is asking for.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets.

```text
I'm a real estate agent explaining a contract term to a client. Write a short,
plain-language explanation of the term below. Keep it under 120 words.

Term or clause: [paste the exact clause, or name the contingency — e.g.
"inspection contingency," "financing contingency," "escalation clause"]

Client's situation: [e.g. "first-time buyer reviewing an offer they're about
to sign" or "seller evaluating a buyer's counter-offer"]

Reading level: [plain and simple / clear and confident / conversational]

Instructions:
- Explain what the term does in practice, not just what it says.
- Use a simple analogy if it helps the concept land.
- Do NOT invent specific deadlines, dollar amounts, or legal conclusions.
- Do NOT give legal advice or say what the client should do.
- End with one sentence the agent can use to transition the conversation.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — the raw contract language:

> This agreement is contingent upon Buyer obtaining a written commitment for a mortgage loan in the amount of $________ at an interest rate not to exceed ____% per annum, amortized over __ years, within ____ days of the acceptance date of this contract. If Buyer is unable to obtain such commitment within the specified time, Buyer shall notify Seller in writing, and this contract shall be null and void and all earnest money shall be returned to Buyer.

**What you get back:**

> A financing contingency is your client's built-in exit if the mortgage falls through. Think of it as a hold on the sale while the lender does their work — the buyer has an agreed number of days to get a written loan commitment. If the bank says no, the buyer notifies the seller in writing, the deal ends, and the earnest money comes back. If the deadline passes without a commitment and without written notice, the buyer could lose that protection. It's the clause that keeps a buyer from being trapped in a contract they can't fund. Ready to walk through the timeline together?

The before is what clients get when they read alone. The after is what they hear when you explain it.

## Make it yours

- **Swap the clause.** The prompt works on any term — escalation clauses, appraisal contingencies, as-is language, earnest money release conditions. Name it or paste it.
- **Match your client.** A first-time buyer needs "plain and simple." A repeat investor might want "clear and confident." Change the reading level line.
- **Ask for the seller's view.** Add "explain it from the seller's perspective" if your client is the listing side. Same clause, different stakes.
- **Request an analogy only.** Add "give me just the analogy in one sentence" if you already have the explanation and just need a memory hook.

## Watch out for

**An AI explanation is not legal advice — and neither is yours.** Your job is to help clients understand what a term does in plain language. Their attorney's job is to interpret what it means for their specific situation and tell them what to do. If a client needs a definitive read on their rights under a clause, refer them to a real estate attorney. Keep that boundary clear every time.

**You own the accuracy of what goes to the client.** The AI describes contingencies correctly in general — but it doesn't know your client's actual deadline, dollar figure, or state law. Review every output against the actual contract before you share it. Don't let the AI invent a detail that wasn't in the clause you gave it.

**Watch for invented specifics.** If the AI output includes a number, a named deadline, or a legal conclusion that wasn't in your input, delete it. The prompt tells the AI not to do this, but verify anyway. You're the backstop.

pages.title: Just-Listed and Just-Sold Posts
pages.description: Turn a new listing or a recent sale into platform-ready social posts.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've got a new listing or a fresh closing and you need posts for three platforms — yesterday. The facts are in your head (or your CMA notes), but staring at a blank caption field burns time you don't have. Paste the prompt below, fill in your facts, and get platform-ready drafts back.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets. Leave a field blank if it doesn't apply.

```text
You're helping me write social media posts for a real estate listing or recent sale.
Write one post per platform listed below.

Property facts:
- Status: [Just Listed / Just Sold]
- Type: [e.g. 3-bed 2-bath craftsman bungalow]
- City/neighborhood: [e.g. Oak Park, IL — Lincoln Square area]
- Price or sale price: [e.g. listed at $449,000 / sold at $462,000]
- Standout feature: [the one thing that sells it]
- Two or three other details: [e.g. updated kitchen, detached garage, walk to the train]

Platforms: [e.g. Instagram, Facebook, LinkedIn]
Tone: [e.g. warm and neighborhood-focused / upbeat / professional]

Rules:
- Write one post per platform. Respect each platform's character limit:
    Instagram: up to 2,200 characters; up to 5 relevant hashtags.
    X (Twitter): up to 280 characters (free accounts).
    LinkedIn: up to 3,000 characters.
    Facebook: keep it conversational and under 300 characters for feed visibility.
- Lead with the standout feature, not the bed/bath count.
- For Just Listed: close with a call to action (schedule a showing, link in bio, DM me).
- For Just Sold: close with a line for buyers or sellers who want the same result.
- Describe the PROPERTY and its features, never the kind of buyer who should live there.
- Do NOT use wording that signals a preference based on race, color, national origin,
  religion, sex, familial status, or disability.
- Do NOT invent facts I didn't give you — no school ratings, no neighborhood comparisons,
  no made-up walk scores.
- Use ONLY the facts I provided above.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your raw notes after the showing:

> just listed - craftsman bungalow oak park - 3bd 2ba - $449k - updated kitchen w quartz + farmhouse sink 2023 - detached 2 car garage - walk to green line - hardwood floors throughout - great for young professionals or first-time buyers - need posts for insta, facebook, linkedin  <!-- noev: illustrative sample figure in a demo -->

**What you get back:**

> **Instagram**
> That kitchen will stop you mid-tour. ✨ Just listed in Oak Park: a 3-bed, 2-bath craftsman bungalow at $449,000 with a fully updated kitchen — quartz counters, farmhouse sink, all done in 2023. Hardwood floors run room to room, and the detached 2-car garage means you're never circling the block. The Green Line is a short walk. Link in bio for the full tour. DM me to schedule a showing.  <!-- noev: illustrative sample figure in a demo -->
> #OakParkRealEstate #JustListed #ChicagoHomes #CraftsmanHome #OakParkIL

> **Facebook**
> Just listed in Oak Park! This 3-bed craftsman bungalow has a stunning 2023 kitchen, hardwood floors, a 2-car garage, and an easy walk to the Green Line — all at $449,000. DM me or tap the link to schedule a showing.  <!-- noev: illustrative sample figure in a demo -->

> **LinkedIn**
> Excited to share a new listing in Oak Park, IL. This 3-bedroom, 2-bath craftsman bungalow is listed at $449,000 and checks a lot of boxes: a beautifully updated kitchen (quartz counters, farmhouse sink — completed 2023), hardwood floors throughout, a detached 2-car garage, and walkable access to the Green Line. If you or someone you know is exploring the Oak Park market, I'd love to connect. Reach out directly or schedule a showing through the link.  <!-- noev: illustrative sample figure in a demo -->

Two things changed: the kitchen leads instead of the bedroom count, and "great for young professionals or first-time buyers" is gone. That kind of language signals a buyer preference and is a fair-housing risk — the prompt's rule tells the AI to leave it out.

## Know the limits

**Platform character limits (as of mid-2026).** The prompt bakes these in, but they're worth knowing if a draft comes back too long:

- **Instagram**: up to 2,200 characters per caption (Instagram doesn't document the caption limit clearly, so treat that number as approximate). On hashtags, use up to **5**. Instagram is rolling its cap down from 30 to five ([Instagram, 2026](#ev-instagram-hashtag-limit-5-realtors)). A few relevant tags beat a wall of generic ones.
- **X (Twitter)**: **280 characters** for free accounts ([X, 2026](#ev-x-post-character-limit-280-realtors)). Premium subscribers get longer posts, but your audience is mostly on free accounts.
- **LinkedIn**: **3,000 characters** per post ([LinkedIn, 2026](#ev-linkedin-post-character-limit-3000-realtors)).
- **Facebook**: the technical limit is very high, but feeds truncate after a couple of lines. Keep posts short — roughly under 300 characters — so your key line shows without a "See more" tap. Check Facebook's current Help Center for specifics.

Limits can change. If a post gets flagged for length, check the platform's current help docs before tweaking by hand.

**Fair housing applies here too.** Your social posts reach the public, so the Fair Housing Act covers them the same way it covers your MLS description. Don't describe who should buy — describe the home. Avoid "perfect for families," "quiet professional community," "ideal for empty nesters," and similar phrases. Describe rooms, features, and what's nearby. The prompt includes this rule, but read every draft before it goes live.

**Accuracy is on you.** The AI uses only what you give it. If your notes are off — wrong price, wrong year on the update, a feature you're not sure about — those errors go straight into the post. Confirm the facts before you paste them in.

## Make it yours

- **Lock in your tone.** Change the tone line to match how you actually write — "neighborhood storyteller," "crisp and data-forward," "warm and conversational." Run it twice with different tones and pick the stronger opener.
- **Add an image caption.** Append "Also write a 10-word image caption for each platform" to get photo overlay text in the same pass.
- **Save it as a template.** Once the rules read right for you, keep the prompt in a note and swap the facts for each new listing.
- **Ask for variants.** Append "give me two versions of the Instagram post — one under 150 characters" to get a short option for Stories or carousel slides.

## Sources

<!-- generated from validation/evidence.yaml — do not edit; run scripts/build_index.py -->

- **Instagram — *Use hashtags on Instagram — Instagram Help Center (cap reduced from 30 to 5 per Instagram's own announcement)*, 2026.** Instagram will gradually update the number of hashtags that you can include in a caption for a reel or post to five. [View source](https://help.instagram.com/351460621611097){#ev-instagram-hashtag-limit-5-realtors} · verified 2026-06-24 · ⚠ secondary mirror
- **X (formerly Twitter) — *Counting Characters — X Developer Platform*, 2026.** Posts on X can contain up to 280 characters. [View source](https://docs.x.com/fundamentals/counting-characters){#ev-x-post-character-limit-280-realtors} · verified 2026-06-24 · primary
- **LinkedIn — *Post and share updates — LinkedIn Help*, 2026.** The character limit for a post is 3000 characters. [View source](https://www.linkedin.com/help/linkedin/answer/a528176){#ev-linkedin-post-character-limit-3000-realtors} · verified 2026-06-24 · primary

sources.wording: Posts on X can contain up to 280 characters.
The character limit for a post is 3000 characters.
Instagram will gradually update the number of hashtags that you can include in a caption for a reel or post to five.

pages.title: Open-House Promo Kit
pages.description: Generate flyer, email, and social copy to promote an open house.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've got an open house on the calendar and three things to write: a flyer blurb, an email invite, and a social caption. They need to sound like they belong together. Run the prompt once, get all three, then swap in your own open line if needed.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets. The AI fills in nothing — every detail comes from you.

```text
You're helping me promote an open house. Using only the facts I give you,
write three short pieces of copy:

1. A flyer blurb (50–70 words). Lead with the standout feature. End with
   the date, time, and address on their own line.
2. An email invite (subject line + 3–4 sentences). Warm and direct. End
   with the event details.
3. A social caption (under 150 characters). Punchy. Include date and time.

Open-house facts:
- Address: [full address]
- Date and time: [e.g. Saturday, July 12 · 1–3 PM]
- Standout features: [e.g. renovated kitchen, large yard, corner lot]
- One thing worth mentioning: [e.g. easy highway access, quiet street]
- Tone: [e.g. warm / upscale / neighborhood-friendly]

Rules:
- Use ONLY the facts above. Do not invent amenities, directions, or
  neighborhood details I didn't provide.
- Describe the HOME and the EVENT, never the kind of person who should
  attend. Avoid phrases like "perfect for a family," "great for
  professionals," or "safe neighborhood."
- Do not use language that signals a preference or limitation based on
  race, color, national origin, religion, sex, familial status, or
  disability.
- Keep the address, date, and time exactly as I wrote them — do not
  paraphrase or reformat.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your raw notes:

> 42 Birchwood Ln, open house Sat July 12 1-3pm. Kitchen totally redone, big backyard with deck, 3 bed 2 bath. Corner lot, easy to find. Neighborhood is great for families. Upscale but approachable tone.

**What you get back:**

**Flyer blurb:**

> A fully renovated kitchen anchors this 3-bedroom, 2-bath home on a generous corner lot. The large backyard and deck give you serious outdoor space. Come see it in person.
>
> **Open House · Saturday, July 12 · 1–3 PM**
> 42 Birchwood Ln

**Email invite:**

> Subject: Open House This Saturday — 42 Birchwood Ln
>
> If you've been looking for a move-in-ready home with a renovated kitchen and a real backyard, this Saturday is worth your time. We're opening 42 Birchwood Ln from 1 to 3 PM — stop by, take a look, and ask us anything. No appointment needed. We'd love to see you there.

**Social caption:**

> Renovated kitchen. Big deck. Corner lot. Come see it Saturday, July 12 · 1–3 PM — 42 Birchwood Ln.

Three things changed from the raw notes. "Great for families" is gone — that phrase is a fair-housing risk, and the prompt strips it automatically. The date and address appear exactly as you typed them. The standout feature, the kitchen, leads every piece instead of hiding in the middle.

## Make it yours

- **Adjust the tone line.** "Upscale but approachable" gives you a different result than "warm and neighborly." Try both and keep whichever fits.
- **Add a second standout feature.** If the primary suite or the view is the real draw, add it to the list. The prompt leads with the first feature you list.
- **Ask for a text-message version.** Add "4. A text-message invite (under 160 characters)" to the prompt and get a fourth piece in the same run.
- **Save the prompt as a template.** Once the rules read right for your market, save it. Next open house, swap only the facts section.

## Watch out for

**Fair housing is the one you can't get wrong.** Your copy sells the home and the event, not a buyer. The federal Fair Housing Act bars any promotion that signals a preference or limitation based on a protected class. Those are **race, color, national origin, religion, sex, familial status, and disability**. What matters is how an ordinary reader hears it, not what you intended. So avoid phrases like "perfect for a young family," "great for professionals," "safe neighborhood," or "walk to church." Describe the home and the event — never the people. Read every AI draft with that lens before it goes out. The prompt helps, but you're the backstop.

**Accuracy is on you.** The AI must not invent event details or amenities — the prompt says so. Still, confirm the date, time, and address yourself before anything goes live. A wrong open-house time on a flyer or social post is the kind of mistake that follows you.

pages.title: Write a Listing Description
pages.description: Turn property facts into a compelling, fair-housing-compliant listing description.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've got a new listing and a blank description field. You know the facts: beds, baths, the updates, the one feature buyers will love. But turning those facts into copy worth reading takes time you don't have. Use this to get a solid first draft in one pass, then adjust it to fit your voice. It's built for a straightforward resale where you already know the property.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets. Leave a line blank if it doesn't apply.

```text
You're helping me write an MLS listing description. Write a compelling,
accurate description for the property below.

Property facts:
- Type: [e.g. 3-bed 2-bath ranch]
- Square footage: [e.g. 1,850]
- Standout feature: [the one thing buyers will love]
- Recent updates: [e.g. kitchen 2024, roof 2023]
- Other features: [garage, yard, floors, layout]
- Location notes: [nearby amenities — places and transit, not who lives there]
- Tone: [warm / upscale / concise]
- Length: [about 100 words, or your MLS limit]

Rules:
- Describe the PROPERTY and its features, never the kind of person who
  should live there.
- Do NOT use wording that signals a preference or limitation based on race,
  color, national origin, religion, sex, familial status, or disability.
  Avoid phrases like "perfect for a young family," "great for professionals,"
  "safe neighborhood," or "walk to church." Use "primary suite," not "master."
- Use ONLY the facts I gave you. Do not invent square footage, school names,
  ratings, or amenities.
- Lead with the standout feature. End with a line inviting a showing.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your raw notes:

> 3bd 2ba ranch, 1850 sqft, updated kitchen 2024 quartz + stainless, new roof 2023, big fenced yard, 2-car garage, cul-de-sac, hardwood floors, near Lincoln Elementary and the Metra. Perfect starter home for a young family.

**What you get back:**

> Set on a quiet cul-de-sac, this updated 3-bedroom, 2-bath ranch offers 1,850 square feet of single-level living. At its heart is a renovated 2024 kitchen with quartz counters and stainless appliances. Hardwood floors run throughout, and the big items are handled: a new roof went on in 2023. Out back, a large fenced yard and a two-car garage give you room to spread out. Lincoln Elementary and the Metra station sit within easy reach. Move-in ready. Schedule a showing to see it in person.

Two things changed. The kitchen leads instead of the bed count. And "perfect starter home for a young family" is gone — that phrase is a fair-housing risk, and the prompt strips it automatically.

## Make it yours

- **Set your voice.** Change the tone line to match how you write — "warm and neighborly," "crisp and high-end." Run it twice and keep the better opener.
- **Match your MLS.** Set the length to your MLS character limit so you're not trimming by hand.
- **Save it as a template.** Once the rules read right, keep the prompt in a note. Next listing, you only swap the facts.
- **Ask for variants.** Add "give me three versions: one long, one short, one for social" to get a listing, a headline, and a caption from a single run.

## Watch out for

**Fair housing is the one you can't get wrong.** Your description sells the home, not a buyer. Federal law (the Fair Housing Act) bars any listing that signals a preference or limitation based on a protected class. Those are **race, color, national origin, religion, sex, familial status, and disability**. What matters is how an ordinary reader hears it, not what you meant. So "perfect for a young family," "great for a professional couple," "walk to church," and even "safe neighborhood" can all land you in trouble. Describe rooms, features, and places. Never the people. Read every AI draft with this lens before it goes live — the prompt helps, but you're the backstop.

**Accuracy is on you too.** The AI will fill a gap with a guess. Confirm the square footage, the school names, and any "walk to" claim against what you actually know. If you didn't give it a fact, it shouldn't be in the listing.

pages.title: Expired and FSBO Scripts
pages.description: Draft outreach scripts for expired listings and for-sale-by-owner sellers.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You spotted an expired listing or a FSBO sign this morning. You want to reach out — but the wrong tone burns the contact before you even have a conversation. Use this to get a respectful, situation-specific first draft fast. Then shape it to your voice before you pick up the phone or hit send.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets.

```text
You're helping me draft a short outreach script for a real estate prospect.
Write a professional, respectful script I can use for the situation below.

Situation:
- Type: [Expired listing OR FSBO]
- Property: [address or brief description — e.g. "3-bed ranch on Maple St"]
- My angle: [what I bring — e.g. "sold 4 homes in this zip in the past 90 days"]
- Channel: [phone call OR text OR email]
- Tone: [warm / direct / low-pressure]
- Length: [short — under 90 words for a call opener, or one short paragraph for text/email]

Rules:
- Open by acknowledging the seller's situation with empathy, not pity.
- State one specific, credible reason I'm reaching out — no generic "I help
  sellers like you" language.
- Make a single, low-commitment ask (a quick call, a walkthrough, a reply).
- Do NOT pressure, guilt, or imply the seller made a mistake.
- Do NOT promise a specific price or outcome.
- Use only the facts I gave you. Don't invent sold comps or statistics.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your notes before drafting:

> expired last week, 123 oak, 4bd, was listed at 450k, sat 67 days, prob overpriced or bad photos, I sold 3 homes nearby in q1, want to do a call

**What you get back:**

> Hi, this is [Your Name] — I noticed your home on Oak Street came off the market last week. I know that's frustrating, especially after 67 days. I've sold three homes in your area this quarter and I'd love to share what I'm seeing buyers respond to right now. Would you have ten minutes this week for a quick call? No pressure, just a conversation.

The notes had the right raw material. The prompt shapes them into an opener that leads with empathy, cites one specific data point, and makes a single low-commitment ask.

## Make it yours

- **Run both versions.** Ask for an expired script and a FSBO script in the same session — they need different angles. FSBO sellers chose to go it alone, so acknowledge that respect up front.
- **Vary the channel.** A phone opener should be shorter and looser. A text or email can be one tighter paragraph. Set the channel field and let the AI match the format.
- **Test two tones.** Run the prompt twice — once "warm," once "direct" — and keep the opener that fits how you actually talk.
- **Add a follow-up.** Once you have the opener, ask the AI: "Write a short follow-up if they don't respond in three days." That gives you a three-touch sequence from a single session.

## Watch out for

**Check the Do Not Call Registry before you call.** When you call to offer your listing services — which expired and FSBO outreach is — federal rules apply. Under the FTC's Telemarketing Sales Rule, you must scrub your contact list against the National Do Not Call Registry before calling numbers on it. Calling a registered number without consent can expose you to significant fines. Pull your contact list, scrub it at [donotcall.gov](https://www.donotcall.gov), and know the exemptions (an existing business relationship, for example, can apply). If you're unsure, ask your broker or a real estate attorney — the rules have nuance, and the risk is real.

**Texting has its own rulebook.** The prompt offers a text channel, but a cold marketing text is not a call. The Telephone Consumer Protection Act (TCPA) governs marketing texts, not the Do Not Call rules. It generally requires prior express written consent before you text through an automated or bulk system, and the penalties are real. Texting one contact by hand has more latitude, but a bulk-texting or CRM tool needs consent first. When in doubt, check with your broker.

**Frustrated sellers read tone fast.** An expired seller spent weeks showing their home and it didn't work. A FSBO seller made a deliberate choice to go without an agent. Scripts that come across as "I told you so" or "you need me" close doors. The prompt above is built to stay respectful — but read the draft before you use it. If it sounds smug or pushy, prompt again with "make it warmer and less sales-y."

**Never promise what you can't deliver.** The prompt blocks the AI from inventing statistics, but double-check any figure you add yourself. A price estimate, a days-on-market claim, or a "I can get you X" line that you can't back up damages trust before the relationship starts.

pages.title: Neighborhood Farming Letter
pages.description: Draft a farming letter or postcard for a target neighborhood.
pages.body:
## When to use this

You've picked a neighborhood to farm and you want to get a letter or postcard in the door. You know the area and you've got a recent sale to point to. But sitting down to write it takes longer than it should. Use this to get a workable first draft, then tune it to sound like you.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets.

```text
You're helping me write a neighborhood farming letter. Write a warm,
professional letter I can mail to homeowners in a target neighborhood.

Details:
- Neighborhood name: [e.g. Maplewood Estates]
- Recent local sale or market note: [e.g. a home on Birch Lane closed at
  $485,000 last month — the highest sale on that street in two years]
- My angle or value I bring: [e.g. I've closed 7 homes in this zip code
  in the past year]
- Tone: [friendly / professional / conversational]
- Length: [short postcard blurb, about 80 words / full letter, about 200 words]

Rules:
- Address the reader as a homeowner in the neighborhood, not by any
  personal characteristic. Never reference who lives there or imply
  anything about the community's demographics.
- Use ONLY the market facts I provided. Do not invent prices, days on
  market, or any other statistics.
- Do NOT include any number I didn't supply. If a fact field is blank,
  leave it out entirely.
- End with a clear, low-pressure call to action — a free home valuation,
  a quick call, or an invitation to reach out with questions.
- Sign off with: [Your name], [Your brokerage], [Your phone/email]
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your rough notes:

> Need a letter for Maplewood Estates. A home on Birch Lane just closed at $485k — highest on that street in 2 yrs. I've closed 7 homes in this zip in past year. Keep it friendly, not salesy. About 200 words. Want them to call me for a free valuation. <!-- noev: illustrative sample figure in a demo -->

**What you get back:**

> Dear Maplewood Estates Homeowner,
>
> I wanted to share some good news from your neighborhood: a home on Birch Lane just closed at $485,000 — the highest sale on that street in two years. If you've been curious what your home might be worth today, this is a strong data point. <!-- noev: illustrative sample figure in a demo -->
>
> I've closed seven homes in this zip code over the past year, so I know what buyers are paying attention to and what's moving. Whether you're thinking about selling now, in a year, or just want to stay informed, I'd love to be your go-to resource.
>
> I'm offering a free, no-obligation home valuation for Maplewood Estates homeowners this month. No pressure — just a clear picture of where you stand. Reach out anytime.
>
> [Your name]
> [Your brokerage]
> [Your phone/email]

## Make it yours

- **Use your actual recent sale.** The more specific the address and price, the more the letter reads as local knowledge, not a mail-merge. Neighbors notice a Birch Lane sale because they know that street.
- **Adjust the call to action.** Swap the free valuation for a market report, a neighborhood stats sheet, or a coffee invite — whatever fits your farm strategy.
- **Try a postcard version.** Set length to "short postcard blurb, about 80 words" and you get a tighter version for a smaller format. Run both and pick whichever fits the format.
- **Save the prompt.** Once the tone reads like you, keep it. Next mailing, you only swap the neighborhood name and the recent sale.

## Watch out for

**Any market number you share is yours to stand behind.** The AI uses only what you give it — so if you say "$485,000," that number goes in the letter. Make sure it's accurate before it hits the mail. Never fill in a sale price or days-on-market figure from memory; pull it from MLS data first. A wrong number in a farming letter can undercut your credibility with the exact homeowners you're prospecting. <!-- noev: illustrative sample figure in a demo -->

**Geographic targeting can raise fair-housing concerns.** Choosing which neighborhood to farm isn't automatically neutral. The protected classes include race, color, national origin, religion, sex, familial status, and disability. Selecting a farm area that tracks any of those can look like steering, even if that wasn't your intent. Farm by geography and market activity: boundaries, turnover rate, price point. Not by who lives there.

**Solicitation rules vary by area.** Do-not-mail and do-not-contact rules differ by state and municipality. If you plan to follow up a mailed letter with a call or door-knock, check the applicable do-not-call rules for your area before you dial. When in doubt, your broker or a real estate attorney can point you to the right local guidance.

pages.title: Sphere and Referral Asks
pages.description: Write messages to your sphere that ask for referrals without being pushy.
pages.body:
## When to use this

Past clients, neighbors, friends — people who already like you and would happily send a referral if you came to mind. The tricky part is asking without making it feel like a pitch. Use this to write a message that's warm, specific, and easy to say yes to.

## The prompt

Paste this into ChatGPT, Claude, or Gemini. Fill in the brackets. Leave a line blank if it doesn't apply.

```text
You're helping me write a short referral-ask message to someone in my sphere.
Write a warm, natural message that makes it easy for them to think of me —
without feeling like a sales pitch.

About the relationship:
- Who I'm writing to: [e.g. past client from 2022, neighbor, college friend]
- Last time we talked: [e.g. ran into them at the block party last summer]
- Any recent touchpoint or occasion: [e.g. they just liked my post, holidays,
  a local event, their home anniversary]

What I want to say:
- The ask: [e.g. "If you know anyone thinking about buying or selling, I'd
  love to help"]
- Anything I want to mention about my work: [e.g. slow market right now but
  I'm active, I just closed one down the street]
- Optional gift or offer: [e.g. I'm sending a market update, I'll do a free
  CMA]

Format:
- Medium: [text message / short email / handwritten note]
- Tone: [warm and casual / professional and friendly / genuine and brief]
- Length: [2–3 sentences for text, short paragraph for email]

Rules:
- Do NOT use hype phrases like "hot market," "now's the time," or "don't
  miss out."
- Keep it human — it should sound like me, not a script.
- The ask should be a soft door, not a hard pitch. The reader should feel
  good about hearing from me.
- Use ONLY the context I gave you. Don't invent shared history.
```

## Before and after

**What you start with** — your rough idea:

> Hey Sarah, hope you're doing well! Just reaching out to see if you know anyone who might be looking to buy or sell. I'm always looking for referrals and would love to grow my business. Feel free to send anyone my way!

**What you get back:**

> Hey Sarah! I was just thinking about the Maple Street house — hard to believe that was almost two years ago. I hope you guys are settling into fall well. I'm still out here helping folks in the area, so if you ever hear of anyone thinking about making a move, I'd be so grateful if you'd pass my name along. No pressure at all — I just love getting to work with people you know.

The before asks Sarah to do something for your business. The after reminds her you two have history, and the ask lands as a favor between friends.

## Make it yours

- **Use a real touchpoint.** The more specific the occasion — a home anniversary, a neighborhood event, a social post they liked — the less it reads as a blast.
- **Vary the format.** Run it as a text for close contacts, a short email for older clients, and a note prompt if you're mailing cards.
- **Send in batches, not blasts.** Pick five people a week. A quick personalized message lands better than anything sent to your whole list at once.
- **Save the base prompt.** Once the tone sounds like you, keep it. Swap out the relationship and occasion for each send.

pages.title: Build vs. Buy
pages.description: How to decide whether to build a capability yourself or buy it off the shelf — the options, the tradeoffs, and when each one wins.
pages.body:
## The decision

You need a capability — a tool, a system, a feature — and you can either build it yourself or buy something that already exists. This guide helps you make that call. It's faced by anyone choosing how to get a capability: a founder, a team lead, or a leader signing off on the spend.

## Does this apply to you?

Use this when **both paths are genuinely real** — a credible off-the-shelf option exists, *and* building it yourself is feasible. If no product fits your need, or you have no way to build and maintain one, you don't really have this decision. It also applies to the middle ground: buying a base and extending it.

## The options

Most real choices sit on a spectrum, not a binary:

- **Buy** — adopt an existing product or service (a SaaS tool, a vendor, an off-the-shelf system), and configure it to your needs.
- **Build** — create it yourself, with your own team or contractors.
- **Buy and extend** — take a commodity base and add your own layer on top, where your edge is in the integration or configuration.

## What actually matters

A handful of criteria should drive this. Most other considerations are noise:

- **Is it your differentiator?** Does this capability set you apart from competitors, or is it just table stakes?
- **Time to value** — how soon you need it working, and what waiting costs you.
- **Total cost of ownership** — the whole lifetime cost, not the sticker price.
- **Control and fit** — how much you need to shape the data, the roadmap, and the details.
- **Risk and reversibility** — how likely each path is to go wrong, and how cheaply you can change your mind.
- **Your capacity** — whether you can staff *and maintain* a build for its whole life, not just ship it.

## How the options compare

**Differentiation.** This is the first filter. Geoffrey Moore's core-vs-context framework is the useful lens ([Moore, 2000](#ev-moore-core-vs-context-2000)). "Core" is what differentiates you and wins customers. "Context" is everything else you must do but that does not set you apart. Buying a capability makes you, at best, as good as everyone else who bought it — fine for context, fatal for core. Building lets you differentiate — but only pays off if the thing actually is a differentiator.

**Speed.** Buying gets you to value in days or weeks. Building takes months, and usually longer than you think.

**Cost over time.** Buying is a predictable subscription — but it runs forever. Building is a large upfront cost plus a hidden majority. Maintenance typically consumes **40 to 80 percent of a software system's total lifetime cost — about 60 percent on average** ([Glass, 2003](#ev-glass-software-maintenance-2003)). The build does not end at launch; that is where most of the bill starts.

**Risk.** Building is riskier than it looks. Across more than 5,400 large IT projects, the average ran **45 percent over budget and 7 percent over time** ([McKinsey, 2012](#ev-mckinsey-oxford-it-projects-2012)). They delivered **56 percent less value than predicted**, and **17 percent went so badly they threatened the company's existence**. Buying carries a different risk: dependence on a vendor, and the cost of switching later.

**Control.** Building gives you full control of the data, the roadmap, and the details. Buying hands the roadmap to the vendor — but hands the upkeep burden to them too.

## When the capability is an AI system

The criteria above still apply, but when what you're building or buying is an AI system — an
LLM-powered feature, a fine-tuned model, an agent — a few things about "build" and "buy"
themselves change.

**"Build" often still means renting someone else's model.** Fine-tuning a foundation model
gets you a model you call by ID through the vendor's API — "use this model just like you would
the final fine-tuned model" — not a copy of the weights you can run or move elsewhere
([OpenAI, 2026](#ev-openai-finetuning-2026-api-only-access)). The full control this guide's
"Control" criterion assumes "build" gives you doesn't fully apply until you actually own the
weights.

**Deprecation is a new risk category, not covered by ordinary vendor risk.** A vendor can
retire the base model your fine-tune depends on. OpenAI gives "at least 6 months" notice for
generally available models and "at least 3 months" for specialized variants — but "inference
on fine-tuned models will be disabled only when the underlying base model is deprecated"
([OpenAI, 2026](#ev-openai-deprecations-2026-notice-and-cutoff)). Your system can stop working
with no code change on your end, on a timeline you don't control.

**Cost tends to scale with usage, not with team size.** Buying isn't always the flat,
predictable subscription this guide's "Cost over time" section describes — OpenAI's API, like
most AI APIs, is billed per token, so cost rises directly with how much the system actually
gets used, not with seats ([OpenAI, 2026](#ev-openai-pricing-2026-per-token)).

**Both paths carry an ongoing evaluation burden that traditional software doesn't have.** Even
a well-built AI system can produce factually wrong output — Anthropic's own guidance on this is
blunt: "always validate critical information, especially for high-stakes decisions"
([Anthropic, 2026](#ev-anthropic-reduce-hallucinations-2026-standing-risk)). Traditional
software doesn't hallucinate; this is a standing cost that doesn't show up in the maintenance
figure cited above.

## When each one wins

**Buy when:**

- The capability is context, not your differentiator.
- A good-enough product already exists.
- You need it soon, and waiting is expensive.
- You do not have the team to build and, more importantly, maintain it.
- The requirement is common and well-understood, not unusual to you.

**Build when:**

- The capability *is* your competitive differentiation — this is core.
- No product fits, and the gap genuinely matters.
- You have a team that can build it and will still be there to maintain it.
- You need control over data or roadmap that no vendor will give you.

**Buy and extend when:**

- The base is a commodity, but your advantage lives in how you configure or connect it.

## How to decide

Answer these in order, about your own situation:

1. **Is this a differentiator or table stakes?** If it's context, default to buy and stop second-guessing.
2. **Does a good-enough product exist?** If nothing fits, your real choice is build or wait.
3. **How fast do you need it, and what does waiting cost?**
4. **Can you maintain a build for its whole life** — not just ship version one?
5. **How reversible is each path?** A buy you can switch out of cheaply needs little agony; a one-way build needs a lot.

## Watch out for

- **Undercounting the build.** Launch is the small part. Maintenance is the majority of the lifetime cost <!-- noev: Glass, cited under "How the options compare" -->, so a build is a permanent staffing commitment, not a one-time project.
- **Optimism about build risk.** Overruns are the norm, not the exception, and the bad cases are very bad <!-- noev: McKinsey, cited above -->. Budget and plan for it.
- **"We're special."** Most needs are context dressed up as core. Building the commodity thing yourself is the most common way this decision goes wrong.
- **Lock-in on the buy side.** Before you depend on a vendor, check the exit cost, whether you can get your data out, and how healthy the vendor is.
- **Binary thinking.** Build-or-buy is often a false choice — buy-and-extend or a partnership is frequently the better answer.
- **Fine-tuning isn't owning.** If "build" means fine-tuning someone else's foundation model, you're still dependent on that vendor <!-- noev: OpenAI, cited under "When the capability is an AI system" --> — weigh deprecation risk into the build case, not just the buy case.

## Sources

<!-- generated from validation/evidence.yaml — do not edit; run scripts/build_index.py -->

- **Geoffrey Moore — *Living on the Fault Line (core-vs-context; later extended in Dealing with Darwin)*, 2000.** Core is anything that contributes directly to competitive differentiation... Context is everything else — all the things that you or your company or product must do to pass muster but which do not differentiate you. [View source](https://geoffreyamoore.com/book/living-on-the-fault-line/){#ev-moore-core-vs-context-2000} · verified 2026-07-01 · primary
- **Robert L. Glass — *Facts and Fallacies of Software Engineering (Fact 41), Addison-Wesley (ISBN 0-321-11742-5)*, 2003.** Maintenance typically consumes 40 to 80 percent (average, 60 percent) of software costs. Therefore, it is probably the most important life cycle phase of software. [View source](https://www.informit.com/store/facts-and-fallacies-of-software-engineering-9780321117427){#ev-glass-software-maintenance-2003} · verified 2026-07-01 · primary
- **McKinsey & Company with the BT Centre for Major Programme Management, University of Oxford — *Delivering large-scale IT projects on time, on budget, and on value*, 2012.** Large IT projects, on average, run 45 percent over budget and 7 percent over time, while delivering 56 percent less value than predicted. [View source](https://www.mckinsey.com/capabilities/tech-and-ai/our-insights/delivering-large-scale-it-projects-on-time-on-budget-and-on-value){#ev-mckinsey-oxford-it-projects-2012} · verified 2026-07-01 · primary
- **OpenAI — *Supervised fine-tuning (API docs)*, 2026.** Use this model just like you would the final fine-tuned model. [View source](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/guides/supervised-fine-tuning){#ev-openai-finetuning-2026-api-only-access} · verified 2026-07-05 · primary
- **OpenAI — *Model deprecations (API docs)*, 2026.** Fine-tuned models created from these base models are not affected by this deprecation, but you will no longer be able to create new fine-tuned versions with these models... Inference on fine-tuned models will be disabled only when the underlying base model is deprecated. [View source](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/deprecations){#ev-openai-deprecations-2026-notice-and-cutoff} · verified 2026-07-05 · primary
- **OpenAI — *Pricing (API docs)*, 2026.** Tokens are billed at the chosen model's input and output rates. [View source](https://developers.openai.com/api/docs/pricing){#ev-openai-pricing-2026-per-token} · verified 2026-07-05 · primary
- **Anthropic — *Reduce hallucinations (Claude Platform docs)*, 2026.** Even the most advanced language models, like Claude, can sometimes generate text that is factually incorrect or inconsistent with the given context... Always validate critical information, especially for high-stakes decisions. [View source](https://platform.claude.com/docs/en/docs/test-and-evaluate/strengthen-guardrails/reduce-hallucinations){#ev-anthropic-reduce-hallucinations-2026-standing-risk} · verified 2026-07-05 · primary

sources.org: McKinsey & Company with the BT Centre for Major Programme Management, University of Oxford
sources.wording: Large IT projects, on average, run 45 percent over budget and 7 percent over time, while delivering 56 percent less value than predicted.
Maintenance typically consumes 40 to 80 percent (average, 60 percent) of software costs. Therefore, it is probably the most important life cycle phase of software.

Core is anything that contributes directly to competitive differentiation... Context is everything else — all the things that you or your company or product must do to pass muster but which do not differentiate you.

Fine-tuned models created from these base models are not affected by this deprecation, but you will no longer be able to create new fine-tuned versions with these models... Inference on fine-tuned models will be disabled only when the underlying base model is deprecated.
Use this model just like you would the final fine-tuned model
Tokens are billed at the chosen model's input and output rates.

Even the most advanced language models, like Claude, can sometimes generate text that is factually incorrect or inconsistent with the given context... Always validate critical information, especially for high-stakes decisions.

pages.title: How These Guides Work
pages.description: What every decision guide contains, how to read it, and how to use one with your AI assistant.
pages.body:
Every guide in this bundle helps you make one decision well. It does not make the call for you. It gives you the options, the criteria that matter, the tradeoffs with evidence behind them, and the conditions under which each choice wins. Once you know the shape, you can read any guide in a couple of minutes and find the part you need.

## What's in every guide

The same sections, always in this order:

- **The decision** — what you're deciding, in a sentence, and who tends to face it.
- **Does this apply to you?** — when this decision comes up, so you know the guide fits your situation before you read on.
- **The options** — the real choices, each described honestly, without strawmen.
- **What actually matters** — the handful of criteria that should drive the call, not everything you could weigh.
- **How the options compare** — the options against those criteria, with the evidence and sources on the claims that carry weight.
- **When each one wins** — plain "if your situation is X, lean toward Y." This is the part to slow down on.
- **How to decide** — a short aid: the few questions to answer about your own situation, in order.
- **Watch out for** — the common traps, how reversible the choice is, and when to bring in a human expert.

## How to read one

Skim **The options** and **What actually matters** to frame the choice. Then spend your time in **When each one wins** and **How to decide** — that's where the guide meets your situation. The **How the options compare** section is where the sourcing lives, so it's the place to check a claim you want to lean on.

## Using a guide with your AI assistant

This is where a guide earns its keep. Connect this knowledge base to your AI assistant over [MCP](/open-knowledge-format/02-the-potential/01-knowledge-for-agents.md), then ask it to help you decide — "help me think through build vs. buy for our situation." Instead of the confident, unsourced list a generic chatbot produces, your assistant works from the guide. It walks you through the real criteria, weighs the sourced tradeoffs, and applies the "when each wins" logic to what you tell it. You still make the call — but on grounded, checkable ground rather than vibes.

## What a guide is not

A guide is not a recommendation for your specific situation. And it's not a substitute for advice when the stakes are high or the details are unusual. It gives you a well-framed, sourced way to think, dated to when it was written. When a lot rides on the call, use it to get sharp. Then check the load-bearing facts against their sources, and bring in someone who knows your specifics.

pages.title: When to Let AI Do It
pages.description: How to decide whether to hand a task to AI or do it yourself — where AI earns its keep, and how to keep control.
pages.body:
## The decision

You have a task in front of you, and AI could plausibly do some or all of it. The question is how much to hand over — from "AI drafts, I finish" to "AI does the whole thing." This guide helps you make that call task by task, so you know when to hand it off and when to keep it.

## Does this apply to you?

Use this when a task sits on the line between you and AI. The tool could do it, and you're deciding how much to trust it with. If the task is trivial, or clearly beyond what AI can do today, you don't have a decision to make. This is for the tasks in between, where either choice is defensible.

## The options

Think of it as a dial, not a switch:

- **Do it yourself** — no AI, or AI only as a sounding board.
- **AI-assisted** — AI does the grunt work; you steer it and make the final call. Augmentation.
- **Let AI do it, then check** — AI produces the result; you review before it counts.
- **Fully automate** — AI does it with no per-instance review. The far end, and a high bar.

## What actually matters

A few things should drive the call. Most else is noise:

- **Stakes and reversibility** — how bad is it if the output is wrong, and how cheaply can you undo it?
- **Verifiability** — can you check the result is right, and check it faster than doing the work yourself?
- **AI's reliability on this kind of task** — is this something the tool is actually good at?
- **The value of your time** — is doing this yourself the best use of you?
- **Accountability** — who answers for the result? AI cannot.

## How the options compare

**Where AI earns its keep.** First drafts, summaries, reformatting, boilerplate, generating options to react to — low-stakes, easily reversible work whose output you can eyeball in seconds. Here, letting AI do it and skimming the result is a clear win.

**Where to keep your hands on it.** The primary agent-guidance is blunt about this ([OpenAI, 2025](#ev-openai-agents-human-oversight-2025)). Keep a human in the loop for actions that are "sensitive, irreversible, or have high stakes," and when the tool keeps failing. Anything you'll be held to, anything hard to take back, anything riding on current facts or real judgment — stay in the loop.

**The verification trap.** Delegating only saves effort if checking the output is cheaper than producing it. If verifying is as hard as doing the task, handing it to AI does not save work — it just hides the risk. It is the same principle that makes AI agents reliable: check the work against reality instead of trusting it blindly ([Anthropic, 2024](#ev-anthropic-effective-agents-groundtruth-2024)).

**Accountability does not transfer.** You still own what goes out under your name. "The AI did it" is not a defense, so the more you'll answer for it, the more you review it.

## When each one wins

**Let AI do it (with a quick check) when:**

- The task is low-stakes and easy to reverse.
- You can verify the output quickly.
- AI is reliably good at this kind of work.
- Your time is better spent on something else.

**Do it yourself, or stay hands-on, when:**

- The stakes are high or the result is hard to undo <!-- noev: OpenAI oversight triggers, cited above -->.
- You can't check the output cheaply.
- It turns on current facts or judgment that AI gets wrong.
- You'll be held accountable for specifics you have to stand behind.

**AI-assisted — the common middle — when:**

- AI can do the heavy lifting, but the final call, the voice, or the judgment is yours. Draft with AI; decide for yourself.

**Fully automate only when:**

- The task is repetitive, well-bounded, and low-stakes, and you've watched AI do it reliably at scale. Even then, keep a spot-check.

## How to decide

Answer these in order, about the task in front of you:

1. **If it's wrong, how bad is it — and can I undo it cheaply?** High-stakes or one-way → stay hands-on.
2. **Can I check the output faster than I could do it?** No → don't delegate; the verification costs more than it saves.
3. **Is AI actually good at this kind of task?** Unsure → try it on a low-stakes case first.
4. **Is my time better spent elsewhere?** Yes, and the above are green → let AI do it.
5. **Who's accountable?** You → review it before it goes out.

## Watch out for

- **The confident-wrong problem.** AI is most dangerous when it's fluently wrong about something you didn't check. Keep a human check on anything that matters <!-- noev: OpenAI oversight triggers, cited above -->.
- **The verification trap.** Delegating a task you can't cheaply verify doesn't save work — it moves the risk somewhere you're not looking.
- **Over-reliance.** Handing over work you need to stay sharp at has a cost that shows up later, not today.
- **The accountability gap.** You own what goes out under your name; make sure a human has actually read it.
- **One-way doors.** Never fully automate something you can't take back without a human gate in front of it. For the runnable version of that rule, see the read-first [AI Playbooks](/ai-use-cases/ai-playbooks/index.md).

## Sources

<!-- generated from validation/evidence.yaml — do not edit; run scripts/build_index.py -->

- **OpenAI — *A Practical Guide to Building Agents*, 2025.** Two primary triggers typically warrant human intervention: Exceeding failure thresholds... High-risk actions: Actions that are sensitive, irreversible, or have high stakes should trigger human oversight. [View source](https://cdn.openai.com/business-guides-and-resources/a-practical-guide-to-building-agents.pdf){#ev-openai-agents-human-oversight-2025} · verified 2026-07-01 · primary
- **Anthropic — *Building Effective Agents*, 2024.** gain ground truth from the environment at each step (such as tool call results or code execution) to assess its progress... It's also common to include stopping conditions (such as a maximum number of iterations) to maintain control. [View source](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/building-effective-agents){#ev-anthropic-effective-agents-groundtruth-2024} · verified 2026-07-01 · primary
